{{afc comment|1=page target for move is at List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:49, 26 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=page target for move is at [[List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province]] - [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:48, 26 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=page target for move is at List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:48, 26 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Finished the linking in this list, but not all the redlinks converted to interlanguage links[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:18, 19 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Redirect|Three Great Shrines of Aki Province}}{{afc comment|1=Switching over to deprecating the templates. So things might be a bit unexpected.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:01, 18 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=If this is in the format "List of Shikinaisha in X" then I have linked all existing shrines into the table. Otherwise I have not. Translation of the texts will come later as it is a lot less difficult than the tables. Exception if there is another comment explaining the exception below[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:34, 17 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Ooh this is so cool, this is the article on the three great shrines of Aki Province[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]])}}
This article is a list of shrines in Aki Province that are recorded in Volume 9 and Volume 10, "Shinmeichō Jōge", of the ''{{ill|Engishiki|ja|Engishiki}}'' ({{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}). It comprises the shrines known as the so-called {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} and their associated {{ill|Ronsha|ja|論社}}, which are classified under {{ill|Aki Province|ja|安芸国}}.
It also includes those shrines, referred to as {{ill|Shikigeisha|ja|式外社}}, that existed at the time of the compilation of the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}} but were not recorded in the register.
There are only three Shikinaisha in Aki Province. These are called the "Three Great Shrines of [[Aki Province]]" and include [[Hayatani Shrine]], [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Take Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LPq-lhGqzgEC&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=Hiroshima|date=1986|publisher=Hiroshima City|language=en}}</ref>
Take Shrine used to have a higher status than [[Itsukushima Shrine]],<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a" /><ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a">{{Cite web|url=https://mydaytrip.com/discover/location/hayatani-shrine|access-date=2023-12-02|website=mydaytrip.com|title=Discover Hayatani Shrine}}</ref> but due to the support of the [[Taira clan]] it was eclipsed.<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a" /> The two shrines used to simultaneously provide offerings in a single ceremony.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Havens|first1=Norman|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami|last2=Inoue|first2=Nobutaka|date=2006|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University|isbn=978-4-905853-08-4|language=en}}</ref> [[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category:qq]][[sn:List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:安芸国の式内社一覧]] == 式内社 == 『延喜式』神名帳では、大社3座3社（いずれもMyojin Taisha社）を記載。
{{List of Shikinai Shrines/Legend}} {| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff; font-size:85%" ! style="background:#a0f0a0" colspan=4 | {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} ! style="background:#a0f0a0" colspan=3 | Designated Shrines ! style="background:#a0f0a0" rowspan=2 | Compilation |- style="white-space:nowrap;" ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.6em; padding-left:2.6em;" | Shrine Name ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.1em; padding-left:2.1em;" | Reading ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.1em; padding-left:2.1em;" | Rank ! style="background:#a0f0a0;" | Note ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.6em; padding-left:2.6em;" | Shrine Name ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.1em; padding-left:2.1em;" | Location ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.3em; padding-left:2.3em;" | Remarks |- |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''[[:en:Saeki_District,_Hiroshima|Saeki]]''' 2座（並大） |- |'''速谷神社'''||hayatanino||Myojin Taisha||月次新嘗||[[Hayatani Shrine]]||[[広島県]][[廿日市市]]上平良||（安芸国二宮）||[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/490201.html] |- |'''伊都伎島神社'''||itsukishimano||Myojin Taisha||||[[Itsukushima Shrine]]||広島県廿日市市宮島町||安芸国[[一宮]]||[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/490101.html] |- |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''[[Aki District, Hiroshima|Aki]]''' 1座（大） |- |'''多家神社'''||ohoiheno||Myojin Taisha||||[[Take Shrine]]||広島県[[安芸郡 (広島県)|安芸郡]][[府中町]]宮の町||安芸国[[総社]]後継社||[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/490301.html] |}
== Content from Take Shrine == '''Take Shrine''' (多家神社) is a [[Sōja shrine]] in [[Fuchū, Hiroshima (town)|Fuchu]], [[Aki District, Hiroshima]]. <ref name=":1">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=shFDAQAAIAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima|title=The Japan Daily Mail|date=1899|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VB9CAQAAMAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima|title=Japan Weekly Mail|date=1899|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Take Shrine Treasure House - Hiroshimatatemonogatari|url=https://www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp/soshiki_file/tatemonogatari/en/page062.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp}}</ref>
It is a [[Sōja shrine]] so it enshrines all the [[kami]] of the shrines in [[Aki Province|Aki]] [[Provinces of Japan|Province]]. It is located on the site of [[Hiroshima Castle]].<ref name=":0" />
It was mentioned in 927 in the [[Engishiki]] and highly ranked as a [[Myojin Taisha]]..<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://aki-fuchu.jp/english/introduction_e_right.htm|access-date=2023-12-02|website=aki-fuchu.jp}}</ref>
There used to be an earlier [[Soja Shrine]] before it was moved here
According to shrine tradition, the original Take Shrine was established on the site of the [[Takemikazuchi]] Palace (mentioned in the Kojiki, an ancient Japanese text) or Enomiya (mentioned in the Nihon Shoki, another ancient text), where Emperor Jinmu stayed for seven years during his eastern expedition.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":0" /> However the status of the shrine gradually declined over time from being one of the three great shrines, and its location was lost during the [[Edo period]].<ref name=":0" />
Its treasure house is one of the few remaining buildings of [[Hiroshima Castle]].<ref name=":0" /> The Take Shrine's treasure house managed to survive a fire in 1915.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Take Shrine's Treasure House in the Azekura Style -|url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/021.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}}</ref><!--Content taken from [[Sōja (Shinto)]] explaining the concept of it-->
Ichinomiya and Soja are not the same thing but were sometimes combined<ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-11-21|title=Ichinomiya / Sōja {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841|access-date=2023-11-28|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231121195632/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841|archive-date=2023-11-21}}</ref>
{{nihongo|Sōja|総社|}} is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] where the ''[[kami]]'' of a region are grouped together into a single sanctuary. This "region" may refer to a ''[[shōen]]'', village or geographic area, but is more generally referred to a whole [[Provinces of Japan|province]]. The term is also occasionally called "sōsha". The ''sōja'' are usually located near the [[Kokufu|provincial capital]] established in the [[Nara period]] under then ''[[ritsuryō]]'' system, and can either be a newly created shrine, or a designation for an existing shrine. The "sōja" can also be the "[[ichinomiya]]" of the province, which themselves are of great ritual importance.<ref name="Herbert">{{cite book|last1=Herbert|first1=Jean|title=Shinto:At the Fountain-head of Japan|date=2011|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-0-415-59348-9|page=105}}</ref>
Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,<ref name="Bocking">{{cite book|last1=Bocking|first1=Brian|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2016|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1138979079}}</ref>
The first mention of "sōja" appeared in the [[Heian period]], in the diary of Taira no Tokinori, dated March 9, 1099 in reference to the province of [[Inaba]].<ref name="Hardacre">{{cite book|last1=Hardacre|first1=Helen|title=Shinto: A History|date=2016|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0190621711}}</ref>
== Content from Hayatani Shrine == '''Hayatani Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Hatsukaichi, a suburb of [[Hiroshima]].<ref>{{Cite web|date=2019-03-03|title=Hayatani Jinja|url=http://yuugenguide.squarespace.com/shrines/2018/12/20/hayatani-jinja|access-date=2023-12-02|website=Yuugen 幽玄|language=en-US}}</ref> It is a [[Myojin Taisha]]<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QmdRAAAAYAAJ&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=Engi-shiki; Procedures of the Engi Era: Books VI-X|date=1972|publisher=Sophia University|language=en}}</ref> and [[Ichinomiya|Ninomiya]] of [[Aki Province]]<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a">{{Cite web|title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Gokurakuji Mountain -|url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/020.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}}</ref> and is currently designated as a [[Beppyo Shrine]].

It was listed as a [[Myojin Taisha]] before the Engishiki was written, In 811 it and [[Itsukushima]] were both given the rank by the Emperor.<ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-03-10|title=Myōjin {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|access-date=2023-12-02|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230310155140/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|archive-date=2023-03-10}}</ref> People from all over Japan visit Hayatani Shrine to pray for [[Road traffic safety|traffic safety]], especially when buying a new car.<ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a" /> The shrine is also known for purifying the buses and trains of the [[Hiroshima Electric Railway]].
This shrine is one of the
For a long time, travelers on the [[San'yōdō|Sanyo Expressway]] have prayed for safety on their journeys at this shrine, and it is still considered a protector of traffic safety on the modern day [[San'yō Expressway|expressway]].

So things might be a bit unexpected.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:01, 18 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=If this is in the format "List of Shikinaisha in X" then I have linked all existing shrines into the table. Otherwise I have not. Translation of the texts will come later as it is a lot less difficult than the tables. Exception if there is another comment explaining the exception below[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:34, 17 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Ooh this is so cool, this is the article on the three great shrines of Aki Province[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]])}}
This article is a list of shrines in Aki Province that are recorded in Volume 9 and Volume 10, "Shinmeichō Jōge", of the ''{{ill|Engishiki|ja|Engishiki}}'' ({{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}). It comprises the shrines known as the so-called {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} and their associated {{ill|Ronsha|ja|論社}}, which are classified under {{ill|Aki Province|ja|安芸国}}.
It also includes those shrines, referred to as {{ill|Shikigeisha|ja|式外社}}, that existed at the time of the compilation of the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}} but were not recorded in the register.
There are only three Shikinaisha in Aki Province. These are called the "Three Great Shrines of [[Aki Province]]" and include [[Hayatani Shrine]], [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Take Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LPq-lhGqzgEC&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=Hiroshima|date=1986|publisher=Hiroshima City|language=en}}</ref>
Take Shrine used to have a higher status than [[Itsukushima Shrine]],<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a" /><ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a">{{Cite web|url=https://mydaytrip.com/discover/location/hayatani-shrine|access-date=2023-12-02|website=mydaytrip.com|title=Discover Hayatani Shrine}}</ref> but due to the support of the [[Taira clan]] it was eclipsed.<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a" /> The two shrines used to simultaneously provide offerings in a single ceremony.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Havens|first1=Norman|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami|last2=Inoue|first2=Nobutaka|date=2006|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University|isbn=978-4-905853-08-4|language=en}}</ref> [[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category:qq]][[sn:List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:安芸国の式内社一覧]] == 式内社 == 『延喜式』神名帳では、大社3座3社（いずれもMyojin Taisha社）を記載。
{{List of Shikinai Shrines/Legend}} {| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff; font-size:85%" ! style="background:#a0f0a0" colspan=4 | {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} ! style="background:#a0f0a0" colspan=3 | Designated Shrines ! style="background:#a0f0a0" rowspan=2 | Compilation |- style="white-space:nowrap;" ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.6em; padding-left:2.6em;" | Shrine Name ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.1em; padding-left:2.1em;" | Reading ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.1em; padding-left:2.1em;" | Rank ! style="background:#a0f0a0;" | Note ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.6em; padding-left:2.6em;" | Shrine Name ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.1em; padding-left:2.1em;" | Location ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.3em; padding-left:2.3em;" | Remarks |- |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''[[:en:Saeki_District,_Hiroshima|Saeki]]''' 2座（並大） |- |'''速谷神社'''||hayatanino||Myojin Taisha||月次新嘗||[[Hayatani Shrine]]||[[広島県]][[廿日市市]]上平良||（安芸国二宮）||[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/490201.html] |- |'''伊都伎島神社'''||itsukishimano||Myojin Taisha||||[[Itsukushima Shrine]]||広島県廿日市市宮島町||安芸国[[一宮]]||[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/490101.html] |- |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''[[Aki District, Hiroshima|Aki]]''' 1座（大） |- |'''多家神社'''||ohoiheno||Myojin Taisha||||[[Take Shrine]]||広島県[[安芸郡 (広島県)|安芸郡]][[府中町]]宮の町||安芸国[[総社]]後継社||[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/490301.html] |}
== Content from Take Shrine == '''Take Shrine''' (多家神社) is a [[Sōja shrine]] in [[Fuchū, Hiroshima (town)|Fuchu]], [[Aki District, Hiroshima]]. <ref name=":1">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=shFDAQAAIAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima|title=The Japan Daily Mail|date=1899|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VB9CAQAAMAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima|title=Japan Weekly Mail|date=1899|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Take Shrine Treasure House - Hiroshimatatemonogatari|url=https://www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp/soshiki_file/tatemonogatari/en/page062.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp}}</ref>
It is a [[Sōja shrine]] so it enshrines all the [[kami]] of the shrines in [[Aki Province|Aki]] [[Provinces of Japan|Province]]. It is located on the site of [[Hiroshima Castle]].<ref name=":0" />
It was mentioned in 927 in the [[Engishiki]] and highly ranked as a [[Myojin Taisha]]..<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://aki-fuchu.jp/english/introduction_e_right.htm|access-date=2023-12-02|website=aki-fuchu.jp}}</ref>
There used to be an earlier [[Soja Shrine]] before it was moved here
According to shrine tradition, the original Take Shrine was established on the site of the [[Takemikazuchi]] Palace (mentioned in the Kojiki, an ancient Japanese text) or Enomiya (mentioned in the Nihon Shoki, another ancient text), where Emperor Jinmu stayed for seven years during his eastern expedition.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":0" /> However the status of the shrine gradually declined over time from being one of the three great shrines, and its location was lost during the [[Edo period]].<ref name=":0" />
Its treasure house is one of the few remaining buildings of [[Hiroshima Castle]].<ref name=":0" /> The Take Shrine's treasure house managed to survive a fire in 1915.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Take Shrine's Treasure House in the Azekura Style -|url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/021.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}}</ref><!--Content taken from [[Sōja (Shinto)]] explaining the concept of it-->
Ichinomiya and Soja are not the same thing but were sometimes combined<ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-11-21|title=Ichinomiya / Sōja {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841|access-date=2023-11-28|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231121195632/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841|archive-date=2023-11-21}}</ref>
{{nihongo|Sōja|総社|}} is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] where the ''[[kami]]'' of a region are grouped together into a single sanctuary. This "region" may refer to a ''[[shōen]]'', village or geographic area, but is more generally referred to a whole [[Provinces of Japan|province]]. The term is also occasionally called "sōsha". The ''sōja'' are usually located near the [[Kokufu|provincial capital]] established in the [[Nara period]] under then ''[[ritsuryō]]'' system, and can either be a newly created shrine, or a designation for an existing shrine. The "sōja" can also be the "[[ichinomiya]]" of the province, which themselves are of great ritual importance.<ref name="Herbert">{{cite book|last1=Herbert|first1=Jean|title=Shinto:At the Fountain-head of Japan|date=2011|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-0-415-59348-9|page=105}}</ref>
Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,<ref name="Bocking">{{cite book|last1=Bocking|first1=Brian|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2016|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1138979079}}</ref>
The first mention of "sōja" appeared in the [[Heian period]], in the diary of Taira no Tokinori, dated March 9, 1099 in reference to the province of [[Inaba]].<ref name="Hardacre">{{cite book|last1=Hardacre|first1=Helen|title=Shinto: A History|date=2016|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0190621711}}</ref>
== Content from Hayatani Shrine == '''Hayatani Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Hatsukaichi, a suburb of [[Hiroshima]].<ref>{{Cite web|date=2019-03-03|title=Hayatani Jinja|url=http://yuugenguide.squarespace.com/shrines/2018/12/20/hayatani-jinja|access-date=2023-12-02|website=Yuugen 幽玄|language=en-US}}</ref> It is a [[Myojin Taisha]]<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QmdRAAAAYAAJ&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=Engi-shiki; Procedures of the Engi Era: Books VI-X|date=1972|publisher=Sophia University|language=en}}</ref> and [[Ichinomiya|Ninomiya]] of [[Aki Province]]<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a">{{Cite web|title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Gokurakuji Mountain -|url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/020.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}}</ref> and is currently designated as a [[Beppyo Shrine]].
== Overview == Hayatani Shrine is a sacred place in [[Aki Province]] that has been worshipped for over 1,700 years. It was listed as a [[Myojin Taisha]] before the Engishiki was written, In 811 it and [[Itsukushima]] were both given the rank by the Emperor.<ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-03-10|title=Myōjin {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|access-date=2023-12-02|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230310155140/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|archive-date=2023-03-10}}</ref> People from all over Japan visit Hayatani Shrine to pray for [[Road traffic safety|traffic safety]], especially when buying a new car.<ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a" /> The shrine is also known for purifying the buses and trains of the [[Hiroshima Electric Railway]].
This shrine is one of the
For a long time, travelers on the [[San'yōdō|Sanyo Expressway]] have prayed for safety on their journeys at this shrine, and it is still considered a protector of traffic safety on the modern day [[San'yō Expressway|expressway]].

It comprises the shrines known as the so-called {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} and their associated {{ill|Ronsha|ja|論社}}, which are classified under {{ill|Aki Province|ja|安芸国}}.
It also includes those shrines, referred to as {{ill|Shikigeisha|ja|式外社}}, that existed at the time of the compilation of the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}} but were not recorded in the register.
There are only three Shikinaisha in Aki Province. These are called the "Three Great Shrines of [[Aki Province]]" and include [[Hayatani Shrine]], [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Take Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LPq-lhGqzgEC&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=Hiroshima|date=1986|publisher=Hiroshima City|language=en}}</ref>
Take Shrine used to have a higher status than [[Itsukushima Shrine]],<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a" /><ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a">{{Cite web|url=https://mydaytrip.com/discover/location/hayatani-shrine|access-date=2023-12-02|website=mydaytrip.com|title=Discover Hayatani Shrine}}</ref> but due to the support of the [[Taira clan]] it was eclipsed.<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a" /> The two shrines used to simultaneously provide offerings in a single ceremony.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Havens|first1=Norman|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami|last2=Inoue|first2=Nobutaka|date=2006|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University|isbn=978-4-905853-08-4|language=en}}</ref> [[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category:qq]][[sn:List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:安芸国の式内社一覧]] == 式内社 == 『延喜式』神名帳では、大社3座3社（いずれもMyojin Taisha社）を記載。

== Content from Take Shrine == '''Take Shrine''' (多家神社) is a [[Sōja shrine]] in [[Fuchū, Hiroshima (town)|Fuchu]], [[Aki District, Hiroshima]]. <ref name=":1">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=shFDAQAAIAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima|title=The Japan Daily Mail|date=1899|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VB9CAQAAMAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima|title=Japan Weekly Mail|date=1899|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Take Shrine Treasure House - Hiroshimatatemonogatari|url=https://www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp/soshiki_file/tatemonogatari/en/page062.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp}}</ref>
It is a [[Sōja shrine]] so it enshrines all the [[kami]] of the shrines in [[Aki Province|Aki]] [[Provinces of Japan|Province]]. It is located on the site of [[Hiroshima Castle]].<ref name=":0" />
It was mentioned in 927 in the [[Engishiki]] and highly ranked as a [[Myojin Taisha]]..<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://aki-fuchu.jp/english/introduction_e_right.htm|access-date=2023-12-02|website=aki-fuchu.jp}}</ref>
There used to be an earlier [[Soja Shrine]] before it was moved here
According to shrine tradition, the original Take Shrine was established on the site of the [[Takemikazuchi]] Palace (mentioned in the Kojiki, an ancient Japanese text) or Enomiya (mentioned in the Nihon Shoki, another ancient text), where Emperor Jinmu stayed for seven years during his eastern expedition.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":0" /> However the status of the shrine gradually declined over time from being one of the three great shrines, and its location was lost during the [[Edo period]].<ref name=":0" />
Its treasure house is one of the few remaining buildings of [[Hiroshima Castle]].<ref name=":0" /> The Take Shrine's treasure house managed to survive a fire in 1915.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Take Shrine's Treasure House in the Azekura Style -|url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/021.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}}</ref><!--Content taken from [[Sōja (Shinto)]] explaining the concept of it-->
Ichinomiya and Soja are not the same thing but were sometimes combined<ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-11-21|title=Ichinomiya / Sōja {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841|access-date=2023-11-28|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231121195632/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841|archive-date=2023-11-21}}</ref>
{{nihongo|Sōja|総社|}} is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] where the ''[[kami]]'' of a region are grouped together into a single sanctuary. This "region" may refer to a ''[[shōen]]'', village or geographic area, but is more generally referred to a whole [[Provinces of Japan|province]]. The term is also occasionally called "sōsha". The ''sōja'' are usually located near the [[Kokufu|provincial capital]] established in the [[Nara period]] under then ''[[ritsuryō]]'' system, and can either be a newly created shrine, or a designation for an existing shrine. The "sōja" can also be the "[[ichinomiya]]" of the province, which themselves are of great ritual importance.<ref name="Herbert">{{cite book|last1=Herbert|first1=Jean|title=Shinto:At the Fountain-head of Japan|date=2011|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-0-415-59348-9|page=105}}</ref>
Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,<ref name="Bocking">{{cite book|last1=Bocking|first1=Brian|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2016|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1138979079}}</ref>
The first mention of "sōja" appeared in the [[Heian period]], in the diary of Taira no Tokinori, dated March 9, 1099 in reference to the province of [[Inaba]].<ref name="Hardacre">{{cite book|last1=Hardacre|first1=Helen|title=Shinto: A History|date=2016|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0190621711}}</ref>
== Content from Hayatani Shrine == '''Hayatani Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Hatsukaichi, a suburb of [[Hiroshima]].<ref>{{Cite web|date=2019-03-03|title=Hayatani Jinja|url=http://yuugenguide.squarespace.com/shrines/2018/12/20/hayatani-jinja|access-date=2023-12-02|website=Yuugen 幽玄|language=en-US}}</ref> It is a [[Myojin Taisha]]<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QmdRAAAAYAAJ&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=Engi-shiki; Procedures of the Engi Era: Books VI-X|date=1972|publisher=Sophia University|language=en}}</ref> and [[Ichinomiya|Ninomiya]] of [[Aki Province]]<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a">{{Cite web|title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Gokurakuji Mountain -|url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/020.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}}</ref> and is currently designated as a [[Beppyo Shrine]].
== Overview == Hayatani Shrine is a sacred place in [[Aki Province]] that has been worshipped for over 1,700 years. It was listed as a [[Myojin Taisha]] before the Engishiki was written, In 811 it and [[Itsukushima]] were both given the rank by the Emperor.<ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-03-10|title=Myōjin {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|access-date=2023-12-02|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230310155140/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|archive-date=2023-03-10}}</ref> People from all over Japan visit Hayatani Shrine to pray for [[Road traffic safety|traffic safety]], especially when buying a new car.<ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a" /> The shrine is also known for purifying the buses and trains of the [[Hiroshima Electric Railway]].
This shrine is one of the
For a long time, travelers on the [[San'yōdō|Sanyo Expressway]] have prayed for safety on their journeys at this shrine, and it is still considered a protector of traffic safety on the modern day [[San'yō Expressway|expressway]].

{{afc comment|1=Used substitition to get all the templates into here relatively easily. On enwiki if you want the templates extract them out of this page to make templates, The jawiki system is too hard[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:35, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=page target for move is at List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:49, 26 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=page target for move is at [[List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province]] - [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:48, 26 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=page target for move is at List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:48, 26 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Finished the linking in this list, but not all the redlinks converted to interlanguage links[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:18, 19 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Redirect|Three Great Shrines of Aki Province}}{{afc comment|1=Switching over to deprecating the templates. So things might be a bit unexpected.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:01, 18 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=If this is in the format "List of Shikinaisha in X" then I have linked all existing shrines into the table. Otherwise I have not. Translation of the texts will come later as it is a lot less difficult than the tables. Exception if there is another comment explaining the exception below[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:34, 17 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Ooh this is so cool, this is the article on the three great shrines of Aki Province[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]])}}
This article is a list of shrines in Aki Province that are recorded in Volume 9 and Volume 10, "Shinmeichō Jōge", of the ''{{ill|Engishiki|ja|Engishiki}}'' ({{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}). It comprises the shrines known as the so-called {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} and their associated {{ill|Ronsha|ja|論社}}, which are classified under {{ill|Aki Province|ja|安芸国}}.
It also includes those shrines, referred to as {{ill|Shikigeisha|ja|式外社}}, that existed at the time of the compilation of the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}} but were not recorded in the register.
There are only three Shikinaisha in Aki Province. These are called the "Three Great Shrines of [[Aki Province]]" and include [[Hayatani Shrine]], [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Take Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LPq-lhGqzgEC&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=Hiroshima|date=1986|publisher=Hiroshima City|language=en}}</ref>
Take Shrine used to have a higher status than [[Itsukushima Shrine]],<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a" /><ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a">{{Cite web|url=https://mydaytrip.com/discover/location/hayatani-shrine|access-date=2023-12-02|website=mydaytrip.com|title=Discover Hayatani Shrine}}</ref> but due to the support of the [[Taira clan]] it was eclipsed.<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a" /> The two shrines used to simultaneously provide offerings in a single ceremony.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Havens|first1=Norman|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami|last2=Inoue|first2=Nobutaka|date=2006|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University|isbn=978-4-905853-08-4|language=en}}</ref> [[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category:qq]][[sn:List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:安芸国の式内社一覧]] == 式内社 == 『延喜式』神名帳では、大社3座3社（いずれもMyojin Taisha社）を記載。
{{List of Shikinai Shrines/Legend}} {| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff; font-size:85%" ! style="background:#a0f0a0" colspan=4 | {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} ! style="background:#a0f0a0" colspan=3 | Designated Shrines ! style="background:#a0f0a0" rowspan=2 | Compilation |- style="white-space:nowrap;" ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.6em; padding-left:2.6em;" | Shrine Name ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.1em; padding-left:2.1em;" | Reading ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.1em; padding-left:2.1em;" | Rank ! style="background:#a0f0a0;" | Note ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.6em; padding-left:2.6em;" | Shrine Name ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.1em; padding-left:2.1em;" | Location ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.3em; padding-left:2.3em;" | Remarks |- |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''[[:en:Saeki_District,_Hiroshima|Saeki]]''' 2座（並大） |- |'''速谷神社'''||hayatanino||Myojin Taisha||月次新嘗||[[Hayatani Shrine]]||[[広島県]][[廿日市市]]上平良||（安芸国二宮）||[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/490201.html] |- |'''伊都伎島神社'''||itsukishimano||Myojin Taisha||||[[Itsukushima Shrine]]||広島県廿日市市宮島町||安芸国[[一宮]]||[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/490101.html] |- |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''[[Aki District, Hiroshima|Aki]]''' 1座（大） |- |'''多家神社'''||ohoiheno||Myojin Taisha||||[[Take Shrine]]||広島県[[安芸郡 (広島県)|安芸郡]][[府中町]]宮の町||安芸国[[総社]]後継社||[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/490301.html] |}
== Content from Take Shrine == '''Take Shrine''' (多家神社) is a [[Sōja shrine]] in [[Fuchū, Hiroshima (town)|Fuchu]], [[Aki District, Hiroshima]]. <ref name=":1">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=shFDAQAAIAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima|title=The Japan Daily Mail|date=1899|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VB9CAQAAMAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima|title=Japan Weekly Mail|date=1899|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Take Shrine Treasure House - Hiroshimatatemonogatari|url=https://www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp/soshiki_file/tatemonogatari/en/page062.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp}}</ref>
It is a [[Sōja shrine]] so it enshrines all the [[kami]] of the shrines in [[Aki Province|Aki]] [[Provinces of Japan|Province]]. It is located on the site of [[Hiroshima Castle]].<ref name=":0" />

There used to be an earlier [[Soja Shrine]] before it was moved here
According to shrine tradition, the original Take Shrine was established on the site of the [[Takemikazuchi]] Palace (mentioned in the Kojiki, an ancient Japanese text) or Enomiya (mentioned in the Nihon Shoki, another ancient text), where Emperor Jinmu stayed for seven years during his eastern expedition.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":0" /> However the status of the shrine gradually declined over time from being one of the three great shrines, and its location was lost during the [[Edo period]].<ref name=":0" />
Its treasure house is one of the few remaining buildings of [[Hiroshima Castle]].<ref name=":0" /> The Take Shrine's treasure house managed to survive a fire in 1915.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Take Shrine's Treasure House in the Azekura Style -|url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/021.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}}</ref><!--Content taken from [[Sōja (Shinto)]] explaining the concept of it-->
Ichinomiya and Soja are not the same thing but were sometimes combined<ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-11-21|title=Ichinomiya / Sōja {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841|access-date=2023-11-28|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231121195632/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841|archive-date=2023-11-21}}</ref>
{{nihongo|Sōja|総社|}} is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] where the ''[[kami]]'' of a region are grouped together into a single sanctuary. This "region" may refer to a ''[[shōen]]'', village or geographic area, but is more generally referred to a whole [[Provinces of Japan|province]]. The term is also occasionally called "sōsha". The ''sōja'' are usually located near the [[Kokufu|provincial capital]] established in the [[Nara period]] under then ''[[ritsuryō]]'' system, and can either be a newly created shrine, or a designation for an existing shrine. The "sōja" can also be the "[[ichinomiya]]" of the province, which themselves are of great ritual importance.<ref name="Herbert">{{cite book|last1=Herbert|first1=Jean|title=Shinto:At the Fountain-head of Japan|date=2011|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-0-415-59348-9|page=105}}</ref>
Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,<ref name="Bocking">{{cite book|last1=Bocking|first1=Brian|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2016|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1138979079}}</ref>
The first mention of "sōja" appeared in the [[Heian period]], in the diary of Taira no Tokinori, dated March 9, 1099 in reference to the province of [[Inaba]].<ref name="Hardacre">{{cite book|last1=Hardacre|first1=Helen|title=Shinto: A History|date=2016|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0190621711}}</ref>
== Content from Hayatani Shrine == '''Hayatani Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Hatsukaichi, a suburb of [[Hiroshima]].<ref>{{Cite web|date=2019-03-03|title=Hayatani Jinja|url=http://yuugenguide.squarespace.com/shrines/2018/12/20/hayatani-jinja|access-date=2023-12-02|website=Yuugen 幽玄|language=en-US}}</ref> It is a [[Myojin Taisha]]<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QmdRAAAAYAAJ&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=Engi-shiki; Procedures of the Engi Era: Books VI-X|date=1972|publisher=Sophia University|language=en}}</ref> and [[Ichinomiya|Ninomiya]] of [[Aki Province]]<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a">{{Cite web|title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Gokurakuji Mountain -|url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/020.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}}</ref> and is currently designated as a [[Beppyo Shrine]].
== Overview == Hayatani Shrine is a sacred place in [[Aki Province]] that has been worshipped for over 1,700 years. It was listed as a [[Myojin Taisha]] before the Engishiki was written, In 811 it and [[Itsukushima]] were both given the rank by the Emperor.<ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-03-10|title=Myōjin {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|access-date=2023-12-02|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230310155140/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|archive-date=2023-03-10}}</ref> People from all over Japan visit Hayatani Shrine to pray for [[Road traffic safety|traffic safety]], especially when buying a new car.<ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a" /> The shrine is also known for purifying the buses and trains of the [[Hiroshima Electric Railway]].
This shrine is one of the
For a long time, travelers on the [[San'yōdō|Sanyo Expressway]] have prayed for safety on their journeys at this shrine, and it is still considered a protector of traffic safety on the modern day [[San'yō Expressway|expressway]].

{{afc comment|1=Used substitition to get all the templates into here relatively easily. On enwiki if you want the templates extract them out of this page to make templates, The jawiki system is too hard[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:35, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=page target for move is at List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:49, 26 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=page target for move is at [[List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province]] - [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:48, 26 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=page target for move is at List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:48, 26 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Finished the linking in this list, but not all the redlinks converted to interlanguage links[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:18, 19 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Redirect|Three Great Shrines of Aki Province}}{{afc comment|1=Switching over to deprecating the templates. So things might be a bit unexpected.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:01, 18 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=If this is in the format "List of Shikinaisha in X" then I have linked all existing shrines into the table. Otherwise I have not. Translation of the texts will come later as it is a lot less difficult than the tables. Exception if there is another comment explaining the exception below[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:34, 17 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Ooh this is so cool, this is the article on the three great shrines of Aki Province[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]])}}
This article is a list of shrines in Aki Province that are recorded in Volume 9 and Volume 10, "Shinmeichō Jōge", of the ''{{ill|Engishiki|ja|Engishiki}}'' ({{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}). It comprises the shrines known as the so-called {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} and their associated {{ill|Ronsha|ja|論社}}, which are classified under {{ill|Aki Province|ja|安芸国}}.
It also includes those shrines, referred to as {{ill|Shikigeisha|ja|式外社}}, that existed at the time of the compilation of the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}} but were not recorded in the register.
There are only three Shikinaisha in Aki Province. These are called the "Three Great Shrines of [[Aki Province]]" and include [[Hayatani Shrine]], [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Take Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LPq-lhGqzgEC&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=Hiroshima|date=1986|publisher=Hiroshima City|language=en}}</ref>
Take Shrine used to have a higher status than [[Itsukushima Shrine]],<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a" /><ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a">{{Cite web|url=https://mydaytrip.com/discover/location/hayatani-shrine|access-date=2023-12-02|website=mydaytrip.com|title=Discover Hayatani Shrine}}</ref> but due to the support of the [[Taira clan]] it was eclipsed.<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a" /> The two shrines used to simultaneously provide offerings in a single ceremony.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Havens|first1=Norman|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami|last2=Inoue|first2=Nobutaka|date=2006|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University|isbn=978-4-905853-08-4|language=en}}</ref>
== 式内社 == 『延喜式』神名帳では、大社3座3社（いずれもMyojin Taisha社）を記載。
{{List of Shikinai Shrines/Legend}} {| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff; font-size:85%" ! style="background:#a0f0a0" colspan=4 | {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} ! style="background:#a0f0a0" colspan=3 | Designated Shrines ! style="background:#a0f0a0" rowspan=2 | Compilation |- style="white-space:nowrap;" ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.6em; padding-left:2.6em;" | Shrine Name ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.1em; padding-left:2.1em;" | Reading ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.1em; padding-left:2.1em;" | Rank ! style="background:#a0f0a0;" | Note ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.6em; padding-left:2.6em;" | Shrine Name ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.1em; padding-left:2.1em;" | Location ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.3em; padding-left:2.3em;" | Remarks |- |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''[[:en:Saeki_District,_Hiroshima|Saeki]]''' 2座（並大） |- |'''速谷神社'''||hayatanino||Myojin Taisha||月次新嘗||[[Hayatani Shrine]]||[[広島県]][[廿日市市]]上平良||（安芸国二宮）||[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/490201.html] |- |'''伊都伎島神社'''||itsukishimano||Myojin Taisha||||[[Itsukushima Shrine]]||広島県廿日市市宮島町||安芸国[[一宮]]||[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/490101.html] |- |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''[[Aki District, Hiroshima|Aki]]''' 1座（大） |- |'''多家神社'''||ohoiheno||Myojin Taisha||||[[Take Shrine]]||広島県[[安芸郡 (広島県)|安芸郡]][[府中町]]宮の町||安芸国[[総社]]後継社||[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/490301.html] |}
== Content from Take Shrine == '''Take Shrine''' (多家神社) is a [[Sōja shrine]] in [[Fuchū, Hiroshima (town)|Fuchu]], [[Aki District, Hiroshima]]. <ref name=":1">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=shFDAQAAIAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima|title=The Japan Daily Mail|date=1899|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VB9CAQAAMAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima|title=Japan Weekly Mail|date=1899|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Take Shrine Treasure House - Hiroshimatatemonogatari|url=https://www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp/soshiki_file/tatemonogatari/en/page062.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp}}</ref>
It is a [[Sōja shrine]] so it enshrines all the [[kami]] of the shrines in [[Aki Province|Aki]] [[Provinces of Japan|Province]]. It is located on the site of [[Hiroshima Castle]].<ref name=":0" />
It was mentioned in 927 in the [[Engishiki]] and highly ranked as a [[Myojin Taisha]]..<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://aki-fuchu.jp/english/introduction_e_right.htm|access-date=2023-12-02|website=aki-fuchu.jp}}</ref>
There used to be an earlier [[Soja Shrine]] before it was moved here
According to shrine tradition, the original Take Shrine was established on the site of the [[Takemikazuchi]] Palace (mentioned in the Kojiki, an ancient Japanese text) or Enomiya (mentioned in the Nihon Shoki, another ancient text), where Emperor Jinmu stayed for seven years during his eastern expedition.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":0" /> However the status of the shrine gradually declined over time from being one of the three great shrines, and its location was lost during the [[Edo period]].<ref name=":0" />
Its treasure house is one of the few remaining buildings of [[Hiroshima Castle]].<ref name=":0" /> The Take Shrine's treasure house managed to survive a fire in 1915.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Take Shrine's Treasure House in the Azekura Style -|url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/021.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}}</ref><!--Content taken from [[Sōja (Shinto)]] explaining the concept of it-->
Ichinomiya and Soja are not the same thing but were sometimes combined<ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-11-21|title=Ichinomiya / Sōja {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841|access-date=2023-11-28|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231121195632/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841|archive-date=2023-11-21}}</ref>
{{nihongo|Sōja|総社|}} is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] where the ''[[kami]]'' of a region are grouped together into a single sanctuary. This "region" may refer to a ''[[shōen]]'', village or geographic area, but is more generally referred to a whole [[Provinces of Japan|province]]. The term is also occasionally called "sōsha". The ''sōja'' are usually located near the [[Kokufu|provincial capital]] established in the [[Nara period]] under then ''[[ritsuryō]]'' system, and can either be a newly created shrine, or a designation for an existing shrine. The "sōja" can also be the "[[ichinomiya]]" of the province, which themselves are of great ritual importance.<ref name="Herbert">{{cite book|last1=Herbert|first1=Jean|title=Shinto:At the Fountain-head of Japan|date=2011|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-0-415-59348-9|page=105}}</ref>
Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,<ref name="Bocking">{{cite book|last1=Bocking|first1=Brian|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2016|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1138979079}}</ref>
The first mention of "sōja" appeared in the [[Heian period]], in the diary of Taira no Tokinori, dated March 9, 1099 in reference to the province of [[Inaba]].<ref name="Hardacre">{{cite book|last1=Hardacre|first1=Helen|title=Shinto: A History|date=2016|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0190621711}}</ref>
== Content from Hayatani Shrine == '''Hayatani Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Hatsukaichi, a suburb of [[Hiroshima]].<ref>{{Cite web|date=2019-03-03|title=Hayatani Jinja|url=http://yuugenguide.squarespace.com/shrines/2018/12/20/hayatani-jinja|access-date=2023-12-02|website=Yuugen 幽玄|language=en-US}}</ref> It is a [[Myojin Taisha]]<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QmdRAAAAYAAJ&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=Engi-shiki; Procedures of the Engi Era: Books VI-X|date=1972|publisher=Sophia University|language=en}}</ref> and [[Ichinomiya|Ninomiya]] of [[Aki Province]]<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a">{{Cite web|title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Gokurakuji Mountain -|url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/020.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}}</ref> and is currently designated as a [[Beppyo Shrine]].
== Overview == Hayatani Shrine is a sacred place in [[Aki Province]] that has been worshipped for over 1,700 years. It was listed as a [[Myojin Taisha]] before the Engishiki was written, In 811 it and [[Itsukushima]] were both given the rank by the Emperor.<ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-03-10|title=Myōjin {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|access-date=2023-12-02|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230310155140/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|archive-date=2023-03-10}}</ref> People from all over Japan visit Hayatani Shrine to pray for [[Road traffic safety|traffic safety]], especially when buying a new car.<ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a" /> The shrine is also known for purifying the buses and trains of the [[Hiroshima Electric Railway]].
This shrine is one of the
For a long time, travelers on the [[San'yōdō|Sanyo Expressway]] have prayed for safety on their journeys at this shrine, and it is still considered a protector of traffic safety on the modern day [[San'yō Expressway|expressway]].

This article is a list of shrines in Aki Province that are recorded in Volume 9 and Volume 10, "Shinmeichō Jōge", of the ''{{ill|Engishiki|ja|Engishiki|12=draft}}'' ({{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}}). It comprises the shrines known as the so-called {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|12=draft}} and their associated {{ill|Ronsha|ja|論社|12=draft}}, which are classified under {{ill|Aki Province|ja|安芸国|12=draft}}.
It also includes those shrines, referred to as {{ill|Shikigeisha|ja|式外社|12=draft}}, that existed at the time of the compilation of the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}} but were not recorded in the register.
There are only three Shikinaisha in Aki Province. These are called the "Three Great Shrines of [[Aki Province]]" and include [[Hayatani Shrine]], [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Take Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LPq-lhGqzgEC&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=Hiroshima|date=1986|publisher=Hiroshima City|language=en}}</ref>
Take Shrine used to have a higher status than [[Itsukushima Shrine]],<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a" /><ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a">{{Cite web|url=https://mydaytrip.com/discover/location/hayatani-shrine|access-date=2023-12-02|website=mydaytrip.com|title=Discover Hayatani Shrine}}</ref> but due to the support of the [[Taira clan]] it was eclipsed.<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a" /> The two shrines used to simultaneously provide offerings in a single ceremony.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Havens|first1=Norman|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami|last2=Inoue|first2=Nobutaka|date=2006|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University|isbn=978-4-905853-08-4|language=en}}</ref>
== 式内社 == 『延喜式』神名帳では、大社3座3社（いずれもMyojin Taisha社）を記載。
{{List of Shikinai Shrines/Legend}} {| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff; font-size:85%" ! style="background:#a0f0a0" colspan=4 | {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}} ! style="background:#a0f0a0" colspan=3 | Designated Shrines ! style="background:#a0f0a0" rowspan=2 | Compilation |- style="white-space:nowrap;" ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.6em; padding-left:2.6em;" | Shrine Name ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.1em; padding-left:2.1em;" | Reading ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.1em; padding-left:2.1em;" | Rank ! style="background:#a0f0a0;" | Note ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.6em; padding-left:2.6em;" | Shrine Name ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.1em; padding-left:2.1em;" | Location ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.3em; padding-left:2.3em;" | Remarks |- |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''[[:en:Saeki_District,_Hiroshima|Saeki]]''' 2座（並大） |- |'''速谷神社'''||hayatanino||Myojin Taisha||月次新嘗||[[Hayatani Shrine]]||[[広島県]][[廿日市市]]上平良||（安芸国二宮）||[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/490201.html] |- |'''伊都伎島神社'''||itsukishimano||Myojin Taisha||||[[Itsukushima Shrine]]||広島県廿日市市宮島町||安芸国[[一宮]]||[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/490101.html] |- |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''[[Aki District, Hiroshima|Aki]]''' 1座（大） |- |'''多家神社'''||ohoiheno||Myojin Taisha||||[[Take Shrine]]||広島県[[安芸郡 (広島県)|安芸郡]][[府中町]]宮の町||安芸国[[総社]]後継社||[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/490301.html] |}
== Content from Take Shrine == '''Take Shrine''' (多家神社) is a [[Sōja shrine]] in [[Fuchū, Hiroshima (town)|Fuchu]], [[Aki District, Hiroshima]]. <ref name=":1">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=shFDAQAAIAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima|title=The Japan Daily Mail|date=1899|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VB9CAQAAMAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima|title=Japan Weekly Mail|date=1899|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Take Shrine Treasure House - Hiroshimatatemonogatari|url=https://www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp/soshiki_file/tatemonogatari/en/page062.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp}}</ref>
It is a [[Sōja shrine]] so it enshrines all the [[kami]] of the shrines in [[Aki Province|Aki]] [[Provinces of Japan|Province]]. It is located on the site of [[Hiroshima Castle]].<ref name=":0" />
It was mentioned in 927 in the [[Engishiki]] and highly ranked as a [[Myojin Taisha]]..<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://aki-fuchu.jp/english/introduction_e_right.htm|access-date=2023-12-02|website=aki-fuchu.jp}}</ref>
There used to be an earlier [[Soja Shrine]] before it was moved here
According to shrine tradition, the original Take Shrine was established on the site of the [[Takemikazuchi]] Palace (mentioned in the Kojiki, an ancient Japanese text) or Enomiya (mentioned in the Nihon Shoki, another ancient text), where Emperor Jinmu stayed for seven years during his eastern expedition.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":0" /> However the status of the shrine gradually declined over time from being one of the three great shrines, and its location was lost during the [[Edo period]].<ref name=":0" />
Its treasure house is one of the few remaining buildings of [[Hiroshima Castle]].<ref name=":0" /> The Take Shrine's treasure house managed to survive a fire in 1915.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Take Shrine's Treasure House in the Azekura Style -|url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/021.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}}</ref><!--Content taken from [[Sōja (Shinto)]] explaining the concept of it-->
Ichinomiya and Soja are not the same thing but were sometimes combined<ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-11-21|title=Ichinomiya / Sōja {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841|access-date=2023-11-28|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231121195632/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841|archive-date=2023-11-21}}</ref>
{{nihongo|Sōja|総社|}} is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] where the ''[[kami]]'' of a region are grouped together into a single sanctuary. This "region" may refer to a ''[[shōen]]'', village or geographic area, but is more generally referred to a whole [[Provinces of Japan|province]]. The term is also occasionally called "sōsha". The ''sōja'' are usually located near the [[Kokufu|provincial capital]] established in the [[Nara period]] under then ''[[ritsuryō]]'' system, and can either be a newly created shrine, or a designation for an existing shrine. The "sōja" can also be the "[[ichinomiya]]" of the province, which themselves are of great ritual importance.<ref name="Herbert">{{cite book|last1=Herbert|first1=Jean|title=Shinto:At the Fountain-head of Japan|date=2011|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-0-415-59348-9|page=105}}</ref>
Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,<ref name="Bocking">{{cite book|last1=Bocking|first1=Brian|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2016|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1138979079}}</ref>
The first mention of "sōja" appeared in the [[Heian period]], in the diary of Taira no Tokinori, dated March 9, 1099 in reference to the province of [[Inaba]].<ref name="Hardacre">{{cite book|last1=Hardacre|first1=Helen|title=Shinto: A History|date=2016|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0190621711}}</ref>
== Content from Hayatani Shrine == '''Hayatani Shrine''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Hatsukaichi, a suburb of [[Hiroshima]].<ref>{{Cite web|date=2019-03-03|title=Hayatani Jinja|url=http://yuugenguide.squarespace.com/shrines/2018/12/20/hayatani-jinja|access-date=2023-12-02|website=Yuugen 幽玄|language=en-US}}</ref> It is a [[Myojin Taisha]]<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QmdRAAAAYAAJ&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=Engi-shiki; Procedures of the Engi Era: Books VI-X|date=1972|publisher=Sophia University|language=en}}</ref> and [[Ichinomiya|Ninomiya]] of [[Aki Province]]<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a">{{Cite web|title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Gokurakuji Mountain -|url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/020.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}}</ref> and is currently designated as a [[Beppyo Shrine]].
== Overview == Hayatani Shrine is a sacred place in [[Aki Province]] that has been worshipped for over 1,700 years. It was listed as a [[Myojin Taisha]] before the Engishiki was written, In 811 it and [[Itsukushima]] were both given the rank by the Emperor.<ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-03-10|title=Myōjin {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|access-date=2023-12-02|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230310155140/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|archive-date=2023-03-10}}</ref> People from all over Japan visit Hayatani Shrine to pray for [[Road traffic safety|traffic safety]], especially when buying a new car.<ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a" /> The shrine is also known for purifying the buses and trains of the [[Hiroshima Electric Railway]].
This shrine is one of the
For a long time, travelers on the [[San'yōdō|Sanyo Expressway]] have prayed for safety on their journeys at this shrine, and it is still considered a protector of traffic safety on the modern day [[San'yō Expressway|expressway]].

On enwiki if you want the templates extract them out of this page to make templates, The jawiki system is too hard[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:35, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=page target for move is at List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:49, 26 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=page target for move is at [[List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province]] - [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:48, 26 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=page target for move is at List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:48, 26 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Finished the linking in this list, but not all the redlinks converted to interlanguage links[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:18, 19 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Redirect|Three Great Shrines of Aki Province}}{{afc comment|1=Switching over to deprecating the templates. So things might be a bit unexpected.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:01, 18 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=If this is in the format "List of Shikinaisha in X" then I have linked all existing shrines into the table. Otherwise I have not. Translation of the texts will come later as it is a lot less difficult than the tables. Exception if there is another comment explaining the exception below[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:34, 17 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Ooh this is so cool, this is the article on the three great shrines of Aki Province[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]])}}
This article is a list of shrines in Aki Province that are recorded in Volume 9 and Volume 10, "Shinmeichō Jōge", of the ''{{ill|Engishiki|ja|Engishiki|12=draft}}'' ({{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}}). It comprises the shrines known as the so-called {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|12=draft}} and their associated {{ill|Ronsha|ja|論社|12=draft}}, which are classified under {{ill|Aki Province|ja|安芸国|12=draft}}.
It also includes those shrines, referred to as {{ill|Shikigeisha|ja|式外社|12=draft}}, that existed at the time of the compilation of the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}} but were not recorded in the register.
There are only three Shikinaisha in Aki Province. These are called the "Three Great Shrines of [[Aki Province]]" and include [[Hayatani Shrine]], [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Take Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LPq-lhGqzgEC&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=Hiroshima|date=1986|publisher=Hiroshima City|language=en}}</ref>
Take Shrine used to have a higher status than [[Itsukushima Shrine]],<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a" /><ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a">{{Cite web|url=https://mydaytrip.com/discover/location/hayatani-shrine|access-date=2023-12-02|website=mydaytrip.com|title=Discover Hayatani Shrine}}</ref> but due to the support of the [[Taira clan]] it was eclipsed.<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a" /> The two shrines used to simultaneously provide offerings in a single ceremony.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Havens|first1=Norman|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami|last2=Inoue|first2=Nobutaka|date=2006|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University|isbn=978-4-905853-08-4|language=en}}</ref>
== 式内社 == 『延喜式』神名帳では、大社3座3社（いずれもMyojin Taisha社）を記載。
{{List of Shikinai Shrines/Legend}} {| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff; font-size:85%" ! style="background:#a0f0a0" colspan=4 | {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}} ! style="background:#a0f0a0" colspan=3 | Designated Shrines ! style="background:#a0f0a0" rowspan=2 | Compilation |- style="white-space:nowrap;" ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.6em; padding-left:2.6em;" | Shrine Name ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.1em; padding-left:2.1em;" | Reading ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.1em; padding-left:2.1em;" | Rank ! style="background:#a0f0a0;" | Note ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.6em; padding-left:2.6em;" | Shrine Name ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.1em; padding-left:2.1em;" | Location ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.3em; padding-left:2.3em;" | Remarks |- |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''[[:en:Saeki_District,_Hiroshima|Saeki]]''' 2座（並大） |- |'''速谷神社'''||hayatanino||Myojin Taisha||月次新嘗||[[Hayatani Shrine]]||[[広島県]][[廿日市市]]上平良||（安芸国二宮）||[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/490201.html] |- |'''伊都伎島神社'''||itsukishimano||Myojin Taisha||||[[Itsukushima Shrine]]||広島県廿日市市宮島町||安芸国[[一宮]]||[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/490101.html] |- |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''[[Aki District, Hiroshima|Aki]]''' 1座（大） |- |'''多家神社'''||ohoiheno||Myojin Taisha||||[[Take Shrine]]||広島県[[安芸郡 (広島県)|安芸郡]][[府中町]]宮の町||安芸国[[総社]]後継社||[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/490301.html] |}

Its treasure house is one of the few remaining buildings of [[Hiroshima Castle]].<ref name=":0" /> The Take Shrine's treasure house managed to survive a fire in 1915.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Take Shrine's Treasure House in the Azekura Style -|url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/021.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}}</ref><!--Content taken from [[Sōja (Shinto)]] explaining the concept of it-->
Ichinomiya and Soja are not the same thing but were sometimes combined<ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-11-21|title=Ichinomiya / Sōja {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841|access-date=2023-11-28|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231121195632/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841|archive-date=2023-11-21}}</ref>
{{nihongo|Sōja|総社|}} is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] where the ''[[kami]]'' of a region are grouped together into a single sanctuary. This "region" may refer to a ''[[shōen]]'', village or geographic area, but is more generally referred to a whole [[Provinces of Japan|province]]. The term is also occasionally called "sōsha". The ''sōja'' are usually located near the [[Kokufu|provincial capital]] established in the [[Nara period]] under then ''[[ritsuryō]]'' system, and can either be a newly created shrine, or a designation for an existing shrine. The "sōja" can also be the "[[ichinomiya]]" of the province, which themselves are of great ritual importance.<ref name="Herbert">{{cite book|last1=Herbert|first1=Jean|title=Shinto:At the Fountain-head of Japan|date=2011|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-0-415-59348-9|page=105}}</ref>
Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,<ref name="Bocking">{{cite book|last1=Bocking|first1=Brian|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2016|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1138979079}}</ref>
The first mention of "sōja" appeared in the [[Heian period]], in the diary of Taira no Tokinori, dated March 9, 1099 in reference to the province of [[Inaba]].<ref name="Hardacre">{{cite book|last1=Hardacre|first1=Helen|title=Shinto: A History|date=2016|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0190621711}}</ref>

It was listed as a [[Myojin Taisha]] before the Engishiki was written, In 811 it and [[Itsukushima]] were both given the rank by the Emperor.<ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-03-10|title=Myōjin {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|access-date=2023-12-02|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230310155140/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|archive-date=2023-03-10}}</ref> People from all over Japan visit Hayatani Shrine to pray for [[Road traffic safety|traffic safety]], especially when buying a new car.<ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a" /> The shrine is also known for purifying the buses and trains of the [[Hiroshima Electric Railway]].

For a long time, travelers on the [[San'yōdō|Sanyo Expressway]] have prayed for safety on their journeys at this shrine, and it is still considered a protector of traffic safety on the modern day [[San'yō Expressway|expressway]].

revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=Used substitition to get all the templates into here relatively easily. On enwiki if you want the templates extract them out of this page to make templates, The jawiki system is too hard[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:35, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=page target for move is at List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:49, 26 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=page target for move is at [[List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province]] - [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:48, 26 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=page target for move is at List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:48, 26 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Finished the linking in this list, but not all the redlinks converted to interlanguage links[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:18, 19 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Redirect|Three Great Shrines of Aki Province}}{{afc comment|1=Switching over to deprecating the templates. So things might be a bit unexpected.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:01, 18 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=If this is in the format "List of Shikinaisha in X" then I have linked all existing shrines into the table. Otherwise I have not. Translation of the texts will come later as it is a lot less difficult than the tables. Exception if there is another comment explaining the exception below[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:34, 17 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Ooh this is so cool, this is the article on the three great shrines of Aki Province[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]])}}
This article is a list of shrines in Aki Province that are recorded in Volume 9 and Volume 10, "Shinmeichō Jōge", of the ''{{ill|Engishiki|ja|Engishiki|12=draft|12=draft}}'' ({{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft|12=draft}}). It comprises three shrines all highly ranked as Myojin Taisha.
All three of these shrines are Myojin Taisha.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web|url=https://aki-fuchu.jp/english/introduction_e_right.htm|access-date=2023-12-02|website=aki-fuchu.jp}}</ref><ref name=":4" /> These are called the "Three Great Shrines of [[Aki Province]]" and include [[Hayatani Shrine]], [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Take Shrine]].<ref name=":4">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LPq-lhGqzgEC&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=Hiroshima|date=1986|publisher=Hiroshima City|language=en}}</ref>
Take Shrine used to have a higher status than [[Itsukushima Shrine]],<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a">{{Cite web|title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Gokurakuji Mountain -|url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/020.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}}</ref><ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a">{{Cite web|url=https://mydaytrip.com/discover/location/hayatani-shrine|access-date=2023-12-02|website=mydaytrip.com|title=Discover Hayatani Shrine}}</ref> but due to the support of the [[Taira clan]] it was eclipsed.<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a" /> The two shrines used to simultaneously provide offerings in a single ceremony.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Havens|first1=Norman|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami|last2=Inoue|first2=Nobutaka|date=2006|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University|isbn=978-4-905853-08-4|language=en}}</ref>
'''Take Shrine''' (多家神社) is the [[Sōja shrine]]. <ref name=":1">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=shFDAQAAIAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima|title=The Japan Daily Mail|date=1899|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VB9CAQAAMAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima|title=Japan Weekly Mail|date=1899|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Take Shrine Treasure House - Hiroshimatatemonogatari|url=https://www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp/soshiki_file/tatemonogatari/en/page062.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp}}</ref>
It is a [[Sōja shrine]] so it enshrines all the [[kami]] of the shrines in [[Aki Province|Aki]] [[Provinces of Japan|Province]]. It is located on the site of [[Hiroshima Castle]].<ref name=":0" />
It was mentioned in 927 in the [[Engishiki]] and highly ranked as a [[Myojin Taisha]]..<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":5">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QmdRAAAAYAAJ&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=Engi-shiki; Procedures of the Engi Era: Books VI-X|date=1972|publisher=Sophia University|language=en}}</ref><!-- I need a citation for itsukushima -->
{{nihongo|Sōja|総社|}} is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] where the ''[[kami]]'' of a region are grouped together into a single sanctuary. This "region" may refer to a ''[[shōen]]'', village or geographic area, but is more generally referred to a whole [[Provinces of Japan|province]]. The term is also occasionally called "sōsha". The ''sōja'' are usually located near the [[Kokufu|provincial capital]] established in the [[Nara period]] under then ''[[ritsuryō]]'' system, and can either be a newly created shrine, or a designation for an existing shrine. The "sōja" can also be the "[[ichinomiya]]" of the province, which themselves are of great ritual importance.<ref name="Herbert">{{cite book|last1=Herbert|first1=Jean|title=Shinto:At the Fountain-head of Japan|date=2011|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-0-415-59348-9|page=105}}</ref>
Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,<ref name="Bocking">{{cite book|last1=Bocking|first1=Brian|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2016|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1138979079}}</ref>
According to shrine tradition, the original Take Shrine was established on the site of the [[Takemikazuchi]] Palace (mentioned in the Kojiki, an ancient Japanese text) or Enomiya (mentioned in the Nihon Shoki, another ancient text), where Emperor Jinmu stayed for seven years during his eastern expedition.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":0" /> However the status of the shrine gradually declined over time from being one of the three great shrines, and its location was lost during the [[Edo period]].<ref name=":0" />
Hayatani Shrine is a sacred place in [[Aki Province]] that has been worshipped for over 1,700 years. It was listed as a [[Myojin Taisha]] before the Engishiki was written, In 811 it and [[Itsukushima]] were both given the rank by the Emperor.<ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-03-10|title=Myōjin {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|access-date=2023-12-02|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230310155140/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|archive-date=2023-03-10}}</ref> People from all over Japan visit Hayatani Shrine to pray for [[Road traffic safety|traffic safety]], especially when buying a new car.<ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a" />
== Shikinaisha == {{List of Shikinai Shrines/Legend}} {| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff; font-size:85%" ! style="background:#a0f0a0" colspan=4 | {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft|12=draft}} ! style="background:#a0f0a0" colspan=3 | Designated Shrines ! style="background:#a0f0a0" rowspan=2 | Compilation |- style="white-space:nowrap;" ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.6em; padding-left:2.6em;" | Shrine Name ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.1em; padding-left:2.1em;" | Reading ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.1em; padding-left:2.1em;" | Rank ! style="background:#a0f0a0;" | Note ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.6em; padding-left:2.6em;" | Shrine Name ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.1em; padding-left:2.1em;" | Location ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.3em; padding-left:2.3em;" | Remarks |- |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''[[:en:Saeki_District,_Hiroshima|Saeki]]''' 2座（並大） |- |'''速谷神社'''||hayatanino||Myojin Taisha<ref name=":5" />||Monthly rice offering||[[Hayatani Shrine]]||[[:en:Hatsukaichi|Hatsukaichi]], a suburb of [[Hiroshima]]<ref>{{Cite web|date=2019-03-03|title=Hayatani Jinja|url=http://yuugenguide.squarespace.com/shrines/2018/12/20/hayatani-jinja|access-date=2023-12-02|website=Yuugen 幽玄|language=en-US}}</ref>||[[Ninomiya]]<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a" />||[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/490201.html] |- |'''伊都伎島神社'''||itsukishimano||Myojin Taisha||||[[Itsukushima Shrine]]||[[:en:Hatsukaichi|Hatsukaichi]], a suburb of [[Hiroshima]]<ref name="cali-2013a">{{Cite book|jstor=j.ctt6wqfhm|title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion|last1=Cali|first1=Joseph|last2=Dougill|first2=John|last3=Ciotti|first3=Geoff|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawai'i Press|isbn=9780824837136}}</ref>||[[Ichinomiya]]||[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/490101.html] |- |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''[[Aki District, Hiroshima|Aki]]''' 1座（大） |- |'''多家神社'''||ohoiheno||Myojin Taisha<ref name=":3" />|| ||[[Take Shrine]]||[[Fuchū, Hiroshima (town)|Fuchu]], [[Aki District, Hiroshima]]||[[Sōja shrine]]<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":0" />||[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/490301.html] |}
== References == <references />{{式内社一覧}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:erroneous transclusions of 安芸国の式内社一覧]] [[Category:安芸国の式内社|*]] [[Category:Lists of Shikinaisha by location]] [[Category:qq]][[en:List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:List of Shikinai Shrines]] [[Category:Shikinai Shrine replacements]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province]] [[ja:安芸国の式内社一覧]] [[sn:安芸国のList of Shikinai Shrines]] [[sn:安芸国の式内社一覧]]
