revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=Used substitition to get all the templates into here relatively easily. On enwiki if you want the templates extract them out of this page to make templates, The jawiki system is too hard[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:35, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=page target for move is at List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:49, 26 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=page target for move is at [[List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province]] - [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:48, 26 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=page target for move is at List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:48, 26 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Finished the linking in this list, but not all the redlinks converted to interlanguage links[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:18, 19 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Redirect|Three Great Shrines of Aki Province}}{{afc comment|1=Switching over to deprecating the templates. So things might be a bit unexpected.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:01, 18 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=If this is in the format "List of Shikinaisha in X" then I have linked all existing shrines into the table. Otherwise I have not. Translation of the texts will come later as it is a lot less difficult than the tables. Exception if there is another comment explaining the exception below[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:34, 17 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Ooh this is so cool, this is the article on the three great shrines of Aki Province[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]])}}
This article is a list of shrines in Aki Province that are recorded in Volume 9 and Volume 10, "Shinmeichō Jōge", of the ''{{ill|Engishiki|ja|Engishiki|12=draft|12=draft}}'' ({{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft|12=draft}}). It comprises three shrines all highly ranked as Myojin Taisha.
All three of these shrines are Myojin Taisha.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web|url=https://aki-fuchu.jp/english/introduction_e_right.htm|access-date=2023-12-02|website=aki-fuchu.jp}}</ref><ref name=":4" /> These are called the "Three Great Shrines of [[Aki Province]]" and include [[Hayatani Shrine]], [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Take Shrine]].<ref name=":4">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LPq-lhGqzgEC&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=Hiroshima|date=1986|publisher=Hiroshima City|language=en}}</ref>
Take Shrine used to have a higher status than [[Itsukushima Shrine]],<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a">{{Cite web|title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Gokurakuji Mountain -|url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/020.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}}</ref><ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a">{{Cite web|url=https://mydaytrip.com/discover/location/hayatani-shrine|access-date=2023-12-02|website=mydaytrip.com|title=Discover Hayatani Shrine}}</ref> but due to the support of the [[Taira clan]] it was eclipsed.<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a" /> The two shrines used to simultaneously provide offerings in a single ceremony.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Havens|first1=Norman|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami|last2=Inoue|first2=Nobutaka|date=2006|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University|isbn=978-4-905853-08-4|language=en}}</ref>
'''Take Shrine''' (多家神社) is the [[Sōja shrine]]. <ref name=":1">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=shFDAQAAIAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima|title=The Japan Daily Mail|date=1899|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VB9CAQAAMAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima|title=Japan Weekly Mail|date=1899|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Take Shrine Treasure House - Hiroshimatatemonogatari|url=https://www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp/soshiki_file/tatemonogatari/en/page062.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp}}</ref>
It is a [[Sōja shrine]] so it enshrines all the [[kami]] of the shrines in [[Aki Province|Aki]] [[Provinces of Japan|Province]]. It is located on the site of [[Hiroshima Castle]].<ref name=":0" />
It was mentioned in 927 in the [[Engishiki]] and highly ranked as a [[Myojin Taisha]]..<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":5">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QmdRAAAAYAAJ&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=Engi-shiki; Procedures of the Engi Era: Books VI-X|date=1972|publisher=Sophia University|language=en}}</ref><!-- I need a citation for itsukushima --> {{nihongo|Sōja|総社|}} is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] where the ''[[kami]]'' of a region are grouped together into a single sanctuary. This "region" may refer to a ''[[shōen]]'', village or geographic area, but is more generally referred to a whole [[Provinces of Japan|province]]. The term is also occasionally called "sōsha". The ''sōja'' are usually located near the [[Kokufu|provincial capital]] established in the [[Nara period]] under then ''[[ritsuryō]]'' system, and can either be a newly created shrine, or a designation for an existing shrine. The "sōja" can also be the "[[ichinomiya]]" of the province, which themselves are of great ritual importance.<ref name="Herbert">{{cite book|last1=Herbert|first1=Jean|title=Shinto:At the Fountain-head of Japan|date=2011|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-0-415-59348-9|page=105}}</ref>
Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,<ref name="Bocking">{{cite book|last1=Bocking|first1=Brian|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2016|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1138979079}}</ref> According to shrine tradition, the original Take Shrine was established on the site of the [[Takemikazuchi]] Palace (mentioned in the Kojiki, an ancient Japanese text) or Enomiya (mentioned in the Nihon Shoki, another ancient text), where Emperor Jinmu stayed for seven years during his eastern expedition.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":0" /> However the status of the shrine gradually declined over time from being one of the three great shrines, and its location was lost during the [[Edo period]].<ref name=":0" />
Hayatani Shrine is a sacred place in [[Aki Province]] that has been worshipped for over 1,700 years. It was listed as a [[Myojin Taisha]] before the Engishiki was written, In 811 it and [[Itsukushima]] were both given the rank by the Emperor.<ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-03-10|title=Myōjin {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|access-date=2023-12-02|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230310155140/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|archive-date=2023-03-10}}</ref> People from all over Japan visit Hayatani Shrine to pray for [[Road traffic safety|traffic safety]], especially when buying a new car.<ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a" />
== Shikinaisha == {{List of Shikinai Shrines/Legend}} {| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff; font-size:85%" ! style="background:#a0f0a0" colspan=4 | {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft|12=draft}} ! style="background:#a0f0a0" colspan=3 | Designated Shrines ! style="background:#a0f0a0" rowspan=2 | Compilation |- style="white-space:nowrap;" ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.6em; padding-left:2.6em;" | Shrine Name ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.1em; padding-left:2.1em;" | Reading ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.1em; padding-left:2.1em;" | Rank ! style="background:#a0f0a0;" | Note ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.6em; padding-left:2.6em;" | Shrine Name ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.1em; padding-left:2.1em;" | Location ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.3em; padding-left:2.3em;" | Remarks |- |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''[[:en:Saeki_District,_Hiroshima|Saeki]]''' 2座（並大） |- |'''速谷神社'''||hayatanino||Myojin Taisha<ref name=":5" />||Monthly rice offering||[[Hayatani Shrine]]||[[:en:Hatsukaichi|Hatsukaichi]], a suburb of [[Hiroshima]]<ref>{{Cite web|date=2019-03-03|title=Hayatani Jinja|url=http://yuugenguide.squarespace.com/shrines/2018/12/20/hayatani-jinja|access-date=2023-12-02|website=Yuugen 幽玄|language=en-US}}</ref>||[[Ninomiya]]<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a" />||[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/490201.html] |- |'''伊都伎島神社'''||itsukishimano||Myojin Taisha||||[[Itsukushima Shrine]]||[[:en:Hatsukaichi|Hatsukaichi]], a suburb of [[Hiroshima]]<ref name="cali-2013a">{{Cite book|jstor=j.ctt6wqfhm|title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion|last1=Cali|first1=Joseph|last2=Dougill|first2=John|last3=Ciotti|first3=Geoff|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawai'i Press|isbn=9780824837136}}</ref>||[[Ichinomiya]]||[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/490101.html] |- |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''[[Aki District, Hiroshima|Aki]]''' 1座（大） |- |'''多家神社'''||ohoiheno||Myojin Taisha<ref name=":3" />|| ||[[Take Shrine]]||[[Fuchū, Hiroshima (town)|Fuchu]], [[Aki District, Hiroshima]]||[[Sōja shrine]]<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":0" />||[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/490301.html] |}
== References == <references />{{式内社一覧}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:erroneous transclusions of 安芸国の式内社一覧]] [[Category:安芸国の式内社|*]] [[Category:Lists of Shikinaisha by location]] [[Category:qq]][[en:List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:List of Shikinai Shrines]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province]] [[ja:安芸国の式内社一覧]] [[sn:安芸国のList of Shikinai Shrines]] [[sn:安芸国の式内社一覧]]

This article is a list of shrines in Aki Province that are recorded in Volume 9 and Volume 10, "Shinmeichō Jōge", of the ''{{ill|Engishiki|ja|Engishiki|12=draft|12=draft}}'' ({{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft|12=draft}}). It comprises three shrines all highly ranked as Myojin Taisha.
All three of these shrines are Myojin Taisha.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web|url=https://aki-fuchu.jp/english/introduction_e_right.htm|access-date=2023-12-02|website=aki-fuchu.jp}}</ref><ref name=":4" /> These are called the "Three Great Shrines of [[Aki Province]]" and include [[Hayatani Shrine]], [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Take Shrine]].<ref name=":4">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LPq-lhGqzgEC&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=Hiroshima|date=1986|publisher=Hiroshima City|language=en}}</ref>
Take Shrine used to have a higher status than [[Itsukushima Shrine]],<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a">{{Cite web|title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Gokurakuji Mountain -|url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/020.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}}</ref><ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a">{{Cite web|url=https://mydaytrip.com/discover/location/hayatani-shrine|access-date=2023-12-02|website=mydaytrip.com|title=Discover Hayatani Shrine}}</ref> but due to the support of the [[Taira clan]] it was eclipsed.<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a" /> The two shrines used to simultaneously provide offerings in a single ceremony.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Havens|first1=Norman|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami|last2=Inoue|first2=Nobutaka|date=2006|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University|isbn=978-4-905853-08-4|language=en}}</ref>
'''Take Shrine''' (多家神社) is the [[Sōja shrine]]. <ref name=":1">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=shFDAQAAIAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima|title=The Japan Daily Mail|date=1899|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VB9CAQAAMAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima|title=Japan Weekly Mail|date=1899|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Take Shrine Treasure House - Hiroshimatatemonogatari|url=https://www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp/soshiki_file/tatemonogatari/en/page062.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp}}</ref>
It is a [[Sōja shrine]] so it enshrines all the [[kami]] of the shrines in [[Aki Province|Aki]] [[Provinces of Japan|Province]]. It is located on the site of [[Hiroshima Castle]].<ref name=":0" />
It was mentioned in 927 in the [[Engishiki]] and highly ranked as a [[Myojin Taisha]]..<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":5">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QmdRAAAAYAAJ&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=Engi-shiki; Procedures of the Engi Era: Books VI-X|date=1972|publisher=Sophia University|language=en}}</ref><!-- I need a citation for itsukushima --> {{nihongo|Sōja|総社|}} is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] where the ''[[kami]]'' of a region are grouped together into a single sanctuary. This "region" may refer to a ''[[shōen]]'', village or geographic area, but is more generally referred to a whole [[Provinces of Japan|province]]. The term is also occasionally called "sōsha". The ''sōja'' are usually located near the [[Kokufu|provincial capital]] established in the [[Nara period]] under then ''[[ritsuryō]]'' system, and can either be a newly created shrine, or a designation for an existing shrine. The "sōja" can also be the "[[ichinomiya]]" of the province, which themselves are of great ritual importance.<ref name="Herbert">{{cite book|last1=Herbert|first1=Jean|title=Shinto:At the Fountain-head of Japan|date=2011|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-0-415-59348-9|page=105}}</ref>
Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,<ref name="Bocking">{{cite book|last1=Bocking|first1=Brian|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2016|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1138979079}}</ref> According to shrine tradition, the original Take Shrine was established on the site of the [[Takemikazuchi]] Palace (mentioned in the Kojiki, an ancient Japanese text) or Enomiya (mentioned in the Nihon Shoki, another ancient text), where Emperor Jinmu stayed for seven years during his eastern expedition.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":0" /> However the status of the shrine gradually declined over time from being one of the three great shrines, and its location was lost during the [[Edo period]].<ref name=":0" />
Hayatani Shrine is a sacred place in [[Aki Province]] that has been worshipped for over 1,700 years. It was listed as a [[Myojin Taisha]] before the Engishiki was written, In 811 it and [[Itsukushima]] were both given the rank by the Emperor.<ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-03-10|title=Myōjin {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|access-date=2023-12-02|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230310155140/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|archive-date=2023-03-10}}</ref> People from all over Japan visit Hayatani Shrine to pray for [[Road traffic safety|traffic safety]], especially when buying a new car.<ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a" />
== Shikinaisha == {{List of Shikinaisha/Legend}} {| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff; font-size:85%" ! style="background:#a0f0a0" colspan=4 | {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft|12=draft}} ! style="background:#a0f0a0" colspan=3 | Designated Shrines ! style="background:#a0f0a0" rowspan=2 | Compilation |- style="white-space:nowrap;" ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.6em; padding-left:2.6em;" | Shrine Name ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.1em; padding-left:2.1em;" | Reading ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.1em; padding-left:2.1em;" | Rank ! style="background:#a0f0a0;" | Note ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.6em; padding-left:2.6em;" | Shrine Name ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.1em; padding-left:2.1em;" | Location ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.3em; padding-left:2.3em;" | Remarks |- |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''[[:en:Saeki_District,_Hiroshima|Saeki]]''' 2座（並大） |- |'''速谷神社'''||hayatanino||Myojin Taisha<ref name=":5" />||Monthly rice offering||[[Hayatani Shrine]]||[[:en:Hatsukaichi|Hatsukaichi]], a suburb of [[Hiroshima]]<ref>{{Cite web|date=2019-03-03|title=Hayatani Jinja|url=http://yuugenguide.squarespace.com/shrines/2018/12/20/hayatani-jinja|access-date=2023-12-02|website=Yuugen 幽玄|language=en-US}}</ref>||[[Ninomiya]]<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a" />||[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/490201.html] |- |'''伊都伎島神社'''||itsukishimano||Myojin Taisha||||[[Itsukushima Shrine]]||[[:en:Hatsukaichi|Hatsukaichi]], a suburb of [[Hiroshima]]<ref name="cali-2013a">{{Cite book|jstor=j.ctt6wqfhm|title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion|last1=Cali|first1=Joseph|last2=Dougill|first2=John|last3=Ciotti|first3=Geoff|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawai'i Press|isbn=9780824837136}}</ref>||[[Ichinomiya]]||[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/490101.html] |- |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''[[Aki District, Hiroshima|Aki]]''' 1座（大） |- |'''多家神社'''||ohoiheno||Myojin Taisha<ref name=":3" />|| ||[[Take Shrine]]||[[Fuchū, Hiroshima (town)|Fuchu]], [[Aki District, Hiroshima]]||[[Sōja shrine]]<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":0" />||[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/490301.html] |}

'''Take Shrine''' (多家神社) is the [[Sōja shrine]]. <ref name=":1">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=shFDAQAAIAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima|title=The Japan Daily Mail|date=1899|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VB9CAQAAMAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima|title=Japan Weekly Mail|date=1899|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Take Shrine Treasure House - Hiroshimatatemonogatari|url=https://www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp/soshiki_file/tatemonogatari/en/page062.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp}}</ref>
It is a [[Sōja shrine]] so it enshrines all the [[kami]] of the shrines in [[Aki Province|Aki]] [[Provinces of Japan|Province]]. It is located on the site of [[Hiroshima Castle]].<ref name=":0" />
It was mentioned in 927 in the [[Engishiki]] and highly ranked as a [[Myojin Taisha]]..<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":5">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QmdRAAAAYAAJ&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=Engi-shiki; Procedures of the Engi Era: Books VI-X|date=1972|publisher=Sophia University|language=en}}</ref><!-- I need a citation for itsukushima --> {{nihongo|Sōja|総社|}} is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] where the ''[[kami]]'' of a region are grouped together into a single sanctuary. This "region" may refer to a ''[[shōen]]'', village or geographic area, but is more generally referred to a whole [[Provinces of Japan|province]]. The term is also occasionally called "sōsha". The ''sōja'' are usually located near the [[Kokufu|provincial capital]] established in the [[Nara period]] under then ''[[ritsuryō]]'' system, and can either be a newly created shrine, or a designation for an existing shrine. The "sōja" can also be the "[[ichinomiya]]" of the province, which themselves are of great ritual importance.<ref name="Herbert">{{cite book|last1=Herbert|first1=Jean|title=Shinto:At the Fountain-head of Japan|date=2011|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-0-415-59348-9|page=105}}</ref>
Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,<ref name="Bocking">{{cite book|last1=Bocking|first1=Brian|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2016|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1138979079}}</ref> According to shrine tradition, the original Take Shrine was established on the site of the [[Takemikazuchi]] Palace (mentioned in the Kojiki, an ancient Japanese text) or Enomiya (mentioned in the Nihon Shoki, another ancient text), where Emperor Jinmu stayed for seven years during his eastern expedition.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":0" /> However the status of the shrine gradually declined over time from being one of the three great shrines, and its location was lost during the [[Edo period]].<ref name=":0" />
Hayatani Shrine is a sacred place in [[Aki Province]] that has been worshipped for over 1,700 years. It was listed as a [[Myojin Taisha]] before the Engishiki was written, In 811 it and [[Itsukushima]] were both given the rank by the Emperor.<ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-03-10|title=Myōjin {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|access-date=2023-12-02|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230310155140/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|archive-date=2023-03-10}}</ref> People from all over Japan visit Hayatani Shrine to pray for [[Road traffic safety|traffic safety]], especially when buying a new car.<ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a" />

revision.text:
{{Redirect|Three Great Shrines of Aki Province}} This is a '''List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province'''. Or the list of shrines in [[Aki Province]] which is present in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}}. On this page we will also cover the {{ill|Shikigeisha|ja|式外社|1=Shikigeisha|12=draft|12=draft}} of [[Aki Province]] which are shrines that are known to have existed at that time due to other historical records, such as the [[Rikkokushi]], but for some reason were excluded from the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}}. We will cover them in a separate list to avoid confusion.[[Category:edited lists]] [[Category:Lists of Shikinaisha by location]]
This article is a list of shrines in Aki Province that are recorded in Volume 9 and Volume 10, "Shinmeichō Jōge", of the ''{{ill|Engishiki|ja|Engishiki|12=draft|12=draft}}'' ({{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|12=draft|12=draft}}). It comprises three shrines all highly Ranked as Myojin Taisha.
All three of these shrines are Myojin Taisha.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web|url=https://aki-fuchu.jp/english/introduction_e_right.htm|access-date=2023-12-02|website=aki-fuchu.jp}}</ref><ref name=":4" /> These are called the "Three Great Shrines of [[Aki Province]]" and include [[Hayatani Shrine]], [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Take Shrine]].<ref name=":4">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LPq-lhGqzgEC&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=Hiroshima|date=1986|publisher=Hiroshima City|language=en}}</ref>
Take Shrine used to have a higher status than [[Itsukushima Shrine]],<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a">{{Cite web|title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Gokurakuji Mountain -|url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/020.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}}</ref><ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a">{{Cite web|url=https://mydaytrip.com/discover/location/hayatani-shrine|access-date=2023-12-02|website=mydaytrip.com|title=Discover Hayatani Shrine}}</ref> but due to the support of the [[Taira clan]] it was eclipsed.<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a" /> The two shrines used to simultaneously provide offerings in a single ceremony.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Havens|first1=Norman|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami|last2=Inoue|first2=Nobutaka|date=2006|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University|isbn=978-4-905853-08-4|language=en}}</ref>
'''Take Shrine''' (多家神社) is the [[Sōja shrine]]. <ref name=":1">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=shFDAQAAIAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima|title=The Japan Daily Mail|date=1899|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VB9CAQAAMAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima|title=Japan Weekly Mail|date=1899|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Take Shrine Treasure House - Hiroshimatatemonogatari|url=https://www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp/soshiki_file/tatemonogatari/en/page062.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp}}</ref>
It is a [[Sōja shrine]] so it enshrines all the [[kami]] of the shrines in [[Aki Province|Aki]] [[Provinces of Japan|Province]]. It is located on the site of [[Hiroshima Castle]].<ref name=":0" />
It was mentioned in 927 in the [[Engishiki]] and highly Ranked as a [[Myojin Taisha]]..<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":5">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QmdRAAAAYAAJ&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=Engi-shiki; Procedures of the Engi Era: Books VI-X|date=1972|publisher=Sophia University|language=en}}</ref> {{nihongo|Sōja|総社|}} is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] where the ''[[kami]]'' of a region are grouped together into a single sanctuary. This "region" may refer to a ''[[shōen]]'', village or geographic area, but is more generally referred to a whole [[Provinces of Japan|province]]. The term is also occasionally called "sōsha". The ''sōja'' are usually located near the [[Kokufu|provincial capital]] established in the [[Nara period]] under then ''[[ritsuryō]]'' system, and can either be a newly created shrine, or a designation for an existing shrine. The "sōja" can also be the "[[ichinomiya]]" of the province, which themselves are of great ritual importance.<ref name="Herbert">{{cite book|last1=Herbert|first1=Jean|title=Shinto:At the Fountain-head of Japan|date=2011|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-0-415-59348-9|page=105}}</ref>
Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,<ref name="Bocking">{{cite book|last1=Bocking|first1=Brian|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|date=2016|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1138979079}}</ref> According to shrine tradition, the original Take Shrine was established on the site of the [[Takemikazuchi]] Palace (mentioned in the Kojiki, an ancient Japanese text) or Enomiya (mentioned in the Nihon Shoki, another ancient text), where Emperor Jinmu stayed for seven years during his eastern expedition.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":0" /> However the status of the shrine gradually declined over time from being one of the three great shrines, and its location was lost during the [[Edo period]].<ref name=":0" />
Hayatani Shrine is a sacred place in [[Aki Province]] that has been worshipped for over 1,700 years. It was listed as a [[Myojin Taisha]] before the Engishiki was written, In 811 it and [[Itsukushima]] were both given the Rank by the Emperor.<ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-03-10|title=Myōjin {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|access-date=2023-12-02|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230310155140/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|archive-date=2023-03-10}}</ref> People from all over Japan visit Hayatani Shrine to pray for [[Road traffic safety|traffic safety]], especially when buying a new car.<ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a" />

revision.text:
'''安芸国の式内社一覧'''（あきのくにのしきないしゃいちらん）は、『[[延喜式]]』第9巻・第10巻「神名帳上下」（[[延喜式神名帳]]）に記載のある[[神社]]、いわゆる「[[式内社]]」およびその[[論社]]のうち、[[安芸国]]に分類されている神社の一覧。
また『延喜式』神名帳の編纂当時に存在したが同帳に記載の無い神社、いわゆる「[[式外社]]」についても付記する。
== 式内社 == 『延喜式』神名帳では、大社3座3社（いずれも名神大社）を記載。
{{式内社一覧/凡例}} {{式内社一覧/header}} |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''[[佐伯郡]]''' 2座（並大） |- |'''速谷神社'''||ハヤタニノ||名神大||月次新嘗||[[速谷神社]]||[[広島県]][[廿日市市]]上平良||（安芸国二宮）||[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/490201.html] |- |'''伊都伎島神社'''||イツキシマノ||名神大||||[[厳島神社]]||広島県廿日市市宮島町||安芸国[[一宮]]||[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/490101.html] |- |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''[[安芸郡 (広島県)|安芸郡]]''' 1座（大） |- |'''多家神社'''||オホイヘノ||名神大||||[[多家神社]]||広島県[[安芸郡 (広島県)|安芸郡]][[府中町]]宮の町||安芸国[[総社]]後継社||[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/490301.html] |}
== 式外社 == {{節stub}} 『延喜式』神名帳の編纂当時に存在したが、同帳に記載の無い神社。
== 参考文献 == * 皇典講究所・全国神職会校訂[{{NDLDC|1442211/160}} 『延喜式 上巻』]（大岡山書店、昭和4年） - 国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション * [{{NDLDC|1273537/37}} 『延喜式 第2』]（日本古典全集刊行会、昭和4年） - 国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション
== 外部リンク == * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/jinjastate49.html 安芸国 式内社3座] - 國學院大學21世紀COEプログラム「神道・神社史料集成」
{{式内社一覧}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:あきのくにのしきないしやいちらん}} [[Category:安芸国の式内社|*]] [[Category:式内社の一覧]]

revision.text:
{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11451199]]} This is a '''List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province''' or the list of shrines in [[Aki Province]] which is present in the {ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}
On this page we will also cover the {ill|Shikigeisha|ja|式外社|1=Shikigeisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikigeisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikigeisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳|comment=enwiki is a redirect} of [[Aki Province]] which are shrines that are known to have existed at that time due to other historical records, such as the [[Rikkokushi]], but for some reason were excluded from the Engishiki list. They are shown in a separate table to avoid confusion.
The ''Engishiki'' counts **3 shikinaisha** in the province – **0 shosha** and **3 taisha**.

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11451199]]
[[:ja:安芸国の式内社一覧]]
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:07, 28 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a '''List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province''' or the list of shrines in [[Aki Province]] which is present in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}
On this page we will also cover the {{ill|Shikigeisha|ja|式外社|1=Shikigeisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikigeisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikigeisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of [[Aki Province]] which are shrines that are known to have existed at that time due to other historical records, such as the [[Rikkokushi]], but for some reason were excluded from the Engishiki list. They are shown in a separate table to avoid confusion.
The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} counts 3 {{ill|Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} in the province – 0 {{ill|Shikinai Shosha|WD=Q134917287}} and 3 {{ill|Shikinai Taisha|WD=Q134917288}}.

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 23:38, 28 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a '''List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province''' or the list of shrines in [[Aki Province]] which is present in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}
On this page we will also cover the {{ill|Shikigeisha|ja|式外社|1=Shikigeisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikigeisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikigeisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of [[Aki Province]] which are shrines that are known to have existed at that time due to other historical records, such as the [[Rikkokushi]], but for some reason were excluded from the Engishiki list. They are shown in a separate table to avoid confusion.
The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} counts 3 {{ill|Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} in the province – 0 {{ill|Shikinai Shosha|WD=Q134917287}} and 3 {{ill|Shikinai Taisha|WD=Q134917288}}.

[[:ja:安芸国の式内社一覧]]
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 23:58, 4 July 2025 (UTC)}} This is a '''List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province''' or the list of shrines in [[Aki Province]] which is present in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}
On this page we will also cover the {{ill|Shikigeisha|ja|式外社|1=Shikigeisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikigeisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikigeisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of [[Aki Province]] which are shrines that are known to have existed at that time due to other historical records, such as the [[Rikkokushi]], but for some reason were excluded from the Engishiki list. They are shown in a separate table to avoid confusion.
The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} counts 3 {{ill|Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} in the province – 0 {{ill|Shikinai Shosha|WD=Q134917287}} and 3 {{ill|Shikinai Taisha|WD=Q134917288}}.
== Shikinaisha (式内社) == {| class="wikitable sortable" ! Global !! Prov !! District !! Name !! Kana !! Rank !! Shrine DB !! Celebration !! Seats !! Subtype !! Parent shrine !! Same as |- | 2575 | 1 | Saeki district | {{ill|Hayatani Shrine|WD=Q11639721}} | ハヤタニノ | Myojin Taisha | [https://jmapps.ne.jp/kokugakuin/det.html?data_id=183120 archive] | Tsukinami-/Niiname-sai | single | — | — | — |- | 2576 | 2 | Saeki district | {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|WD=Q191763}} | イツキシマノ | Myojin Taisha | [https://jmapps.ne.jp/kokugakuin/det.html?data_id=183121 archive] | — | single | — | — | — |- | 2577 | 3 | Aki district | {{ill|Take-jinja|WD=Q11430665}} | たけじんじゃ | Myojin Taisha | [https://jmapps.ne.jp/kokugakuin/det.html?data_id=183122 archive] | — | single | — | — | — |}

On this page we will also cover the {{ill|Shikigeisha|ja|式外社|1=Shikigeisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikigeisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikigeisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of [[Aki Province]] which are shrines that are known to have existed at that time due to other historical records, such as the [[Rikkokushi]], but for some reason were excluded from the Engishiki list. They are shown in a separate table to avoid confusion.
The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} counts 3 {{ill|Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} in the province – 0 {{ill|Shikinai Shosha|WD=Q134917287}} and 3 {{ill|Shikinai Taisha|WD=Q134917288}}.

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11451199]]
[[:ja:安芸国の式内社一覧]]
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:52, 7 July 2025 (UTC)}} This is a '''List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province''' or the list of shrines in [[Aki Province]] which is present in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}
On this page we will also cover the {{ill|Shikigeisha|ja|式外社|1=Shikigeisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikigeisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikigeisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of [[Aki Province]] which are shrines that are known to have existed at that time due to other historical records, such as the [[Rikkokushi]], but for some reason were excluded from the Engishiki list. They are shown in a separate table to avoid confusion.
The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} counts 3 {{ill|Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} in the province – 0 {{ill|Shikinai Shosha|WD=Q134917287}} and 3 {{ill|Shikinai Taisha|WD=Q134917288}}.

[[:ja:安芸国の式内社一覧]]
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:35, 12 July 2025 (UTC)}} This is a '''List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province''' or the list of shrines in [[Aki Province]] which is present in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}
On this page we will also cover the {{ill|Shikigeisha|ja|式外社|1=Shikigeisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikigeisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikigeisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of [[Aki Province]] which are shrines that are known to have existed at that time due to other historical records, such as the [[Rikkokushi]], but for some reason were excluded from the Engishiki list. They are shown in a separate table to avoid confusion.
The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} counts 3 {{ill|Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} in the province – 0 {{ill|Shikinai Shosha|WD=Q134917287}} and 3 {{ill|Shikinai Taisha|WD=Q134917288}}.

==Other version== {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11451199]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Redirect|Three Great Shrines of Aki Province}} This is a '''List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province'''. Or the list of shrines in [[:en:Aki Province|Aki Province]] which is present in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}. On this page we will also cover the {{ill|Shikigeisha|ja|式外社|1=Shikigeisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikigeisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikigeisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of [[:en:Aki Province|Aki Province]] which are shrines that are known to have existed at that time due to other historical records, such as the [[Rikkokushi]], but for some reason were excluded from the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}. We will cover them in a separate list to avoid confusion.
This article is a list of shrines in Aki Province that are recorded in Volume 9 and Volume 10, "Shinmeichō Jōge", of the ''{{ill|Engishiki|ja|Engishiki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ({{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}). It comprises three shrines all highly Ranked as Myojin Taisha.
There are three such listed shrines. All three of these shrines are Myojin Taisha.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web|url=https://aki-fuchu.jp/english/introduction_e_right.htm|access-date=2023-12-02|website=aki-fuchu.jp}}</ref><ref name=":4" /> These are called the "Three Great Shrines of [[:en:Aki Province|Aki Province]]" and include [[Hayatani Shrine]], [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Take Shrine]].<ref name=":4">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LPq-lhGqzgEC&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=Hiroshima|date=1986|publisher=Hiroshima City|language=en}}</ref>
Take Shrine used to have a higher status than [[Itsukushima Shrine]],<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a">{{Cite web|title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Gokurakuji Mountain -|url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/020.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}}</ref><ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a">{{Cite web|url=https://mydaytrip.com/discover/location/hayatani-shrine|access-date=2023-12-02|website=mydaytrip.com|title=Discover Hayatani Shrine}}</ref> but due to the support of the [[Taira clan]] it was eclipsed.<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a" /> The two shrines used to simultaneously provide offerings in a single ceremony.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Havens|first1=Norman|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami|last2=Inoue|first2=Nobutaka|date=2006|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University|isbn=978-4-905853-08-4|language=en}}</ref> [[Itsukushima Shrine]] is the [[Ichinomiya]],<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''", p. 3] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref> and [[Take Shrine]] is the [[Sōja shrine]]. <ref name=":1">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=shFDAQAAIAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima|title=The Japan Daily Mail|date=1899|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VB9CAQAAMAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima|title=Japan Weekly Mail|date=1899|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Take Shrine Treasure House - Hiroshimatatemonogatari|url=https://www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp/soshiki_file/tatemonogatari/en/page062.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp}}</ref>
Hayatani Shrine is a sacred place in [[:en:Aki Province|Aki Province]] that has been worshipped for over 1,700 years. It was listed as a [[:en:Myojin Taisha|Myojin Taisha]] before the Engishiki was written, In 811 it and [[Itsukushima]] were both given the Rank by the Emperor.<ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-03-10|title=Myōjin {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|access-date=2023-12-02|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230310155140/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|archive-date=2023-03-10}}</ref> People from all over Japan visit Hayatani Shrine to pray for [[:en:Road traffic safety|traffic safety]], especially when buying a new car.<ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a" /> == Shikinaisha (式内社) == {{List of Shikinaisha/Legend}} {| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff; font-size:85%" ! style="background:#a0f0a0" colspan=4 | {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} ! style="background:#a0f0a0" colspan=3 | Designated Shrines ! style="background:#a0f0a0" rowspan=2 | Compilation |- style="white-space:nowrap;" ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.6em; padding-left:2.6em;" | Shrine Name ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.1em; padding-left:2.1em;" | Reading ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.1em; padding-left:2.1em;" | Rank ! style="background:#a0f0a0;" | Note ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.6em; padding-left:2.6em;" | Shrine Name ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.1em; padding-left:2.1em;" | Location ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.3em; padding-left:2.3em;" | Remarks |- |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''[[:en:Saeki_District,_Hiroshima|Saeki]]''' 2座（並大） |- |'''速谷神社'''||hayatanino||Myojin Taisha<ref name=":5">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QmdRAAAAYAAJ&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=Engi-shiki; Procedures of the Engi Era: Books VI-X|date=1972|publisher=Sophia University|language=en}}</ref>||Monthly rice offering||[[Hayatani Shrine]]||[[:en:Hatsukaichi|Hatsukaichi]], a suburb of [[:en:Hiroshima|Hiroshima]]<ref>{{Cite web|date=2019-03-03|title=Hayatani Jinja|url=http://yuugenguide.squarespace.com/shrines/2018/12/20/hayatani-jinja|access-date=2023-12-02|website=Yuugen 幽玄|language=en-US}}</ref>||[[:en:Ninomiya|Ninomiya]]<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a" />||[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/490201.html] |- |'''伊都伎島神社'''||itsukishimano||Myojin Taisha||||[[Itsukushima Shrine]]||[[:en:Hatsukaichi|Hatsukaichi]], a suburb of [[:en:Hiroshima|Hiroshima]]<ref name="cali-2013a">{{Cite book|jstor=j.ctt6wqfhm|title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion|last1=Cali|first1=Joseph|last2=Dougill|first2=John|last3=Ciotti|first3=Geoff|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawai'i Press|isbn=9780824837136}}</ref>||[[Ichinomiya]]||[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/490101.html] |- |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''[[Aki District, Hiroshima|Aki]]''' 1座（大） |- |'''多家神社'''||ohoiheno||Myojin Taisha<ref name=":3" />|| ||[[Take Shrine]]||[[:en:Fuchū, Hiroshima (town)|Fuchu]], [[Aki District, Hiroshima]]||[[Sōja shrine]]<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":0" />||[http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/490301.html] |}

==Other version== {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11451199]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Redirect|Three Great Shrines of Aki Province}} This is a '''List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province'''. Or the list of shrines in [[:en:Aki Province|Aki Province]] which is present in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}. On this page we will also cover the {{ill|Shikigeisha|ja|式外社|1=Shikigeisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikigeisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikigeisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of [[:en:Aki Province|Aki Province]] which are shrines that are known to have existed at that time due to other historical records, such as the [[Rikkokushi]], but for some reason were excluded from the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}. We will cover them in a separate list to avoid confusion.
This article is a list of shrines in Aki Province that are recorded in Volume 9 and Volume 10, "Shinmeichō Jōge", of the ''{{ill|Engishiki|ja|Engishiki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ({{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}). It comprises three shrines all highly Ranked as Myojin Taisha.
There are three such listed shrines. All three of these shrines are Myojin Taisha.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web|url=https://aki-fuchu.jp/english/introduction_e_right.htm|access-date=2023-12-02|website=aki-fuchu.jp}}</ref><ref name=":4" /> These are called the "Three Great Shrines of [[:en:Aki Province|Aki Province]]" and include [[Hayatani Shrine]], [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Take Shrine]].<ref name=":4">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LPq-lhGqzgEC&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=Hiroshima|date=1986|publisher=Hiroshima City|language=en}}</ref>
Take Shrine used to have a higher status than [[Itsukushima Shrine]],<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a">{{Cite web|title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Gokurakuji Mountain -|url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/020.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}}</ref><ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a">{{Cite web|url=https://mydaytrip.com/discover/location/hayatani-shrine|access-date=2023-12-02|website=mydaytrip.com|title=Discover Hayatani Shrine}}</ref> but due to the support of the [[Taira clan]] it was eclipsed.<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a" /> The two shrines used to simultaneously provide offerings in a single ceremony.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Havens|first1=Norman|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami|last2=Inoue|first2=Nobutaka|date=2006|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University|isbn=978-4-905853-08-4|language=en}}</ref> [[Itsukushima Shrine]] is the [[Ichinomiya]],<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''", p. 3] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref> and [[Take Shrine]] is the [[Sōja shrine]]. <ref name=":1">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=shFDAQAAIAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima|title=The Japan Daily Mail|date=1899|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VB9CAQAAMAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima|title=Japan Weekly Mail|date=1899|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Take Shrine Treasure House - Hiroshimatatemonogatari|url=https://www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp/soshiki_file/tatemonogatari/en/page062.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp}}</ref>
Hayatani Shrine is a sacred place in [[:en:Aki Province|Aki Province]] that has been worshipped for over 1,700 years. It was listed as a [[:en:Myojin Taisha|Myojin Taisha]] before the Engishiki was written, In 811 it and [[Itsukushima]] were both given the Rank by the Emperor.<ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-03-10|title=Myōjin {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|access-date=2023-12-02|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230310155140/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|archive-date=2023-03-10}}</ref> People from all over Japan visit Hayatani Shrine to pray for [[:en:Road traffic safety|traffic safety]], especially when buying a new car.<ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a" /> == Shikinaisha (式内社) == {| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff; font-size:85%" ! {{translated page|ja|安芸国の式内社一覧|version=56551135|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{List of Shikinaisha}} [[ja:安芸国の式内社一覧]] [[d:Q11451199]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Aki Province]] [[Category:Lists of Shikinaisha by location]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11451199]]
[[:ja:安芸国の式内社一覧]]
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 22:02, 12 July 2025 (UTC)}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11451199]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Redirect|Three Great Shrines of Aki Province}} This is a '''List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province'''. Or the list of shrines in [[:en:Aki Province|Aki Province]] which is present in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}. On this page we will also cover the {{ill|Shikigeisha|ja|式外社|1=Shikigeisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikigeisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikigeisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of [[:en:Aki Province|Aki Province]] which are shrines that are known to have existed at that time due to other historical records, such as the [[Rikkokushi]], but for some reason were excluded from the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}. We will cover them in a separate list to avoid confusion.
This article is a list of shrines in Aki Province that are recorded in Volume 9 and Volume 10, "Shinmeichō Jōge", of the ''{{ill|Engishiki|ja|Engishiki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ({{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}). It comprises three shrines all highly Ranked as Myojin Taisha.
There are three such listed shrines. All three of these shrines are Myojin Taisha.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web|url=https://aki-fuchu.jp/english/introduction_e_right.htm|access-date=2023-12-02|website=aki-fuchu.jp}}</ref><ref name=":4" /> These are called the "Three Great Shrines of [[:en:Aki Province|Aki Province]]" and include [[Hayatani Shrine]], [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Take Shrine]].<ref name=":4">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LPq-lhGqzgEC&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=Hiroshima|date=1986|publisher=Hiroshima City|language=en}}</ref>
Take Shrine used to have a higher status than [[Itsukushima Shrine]],<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a">{{Cite web|title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Gokurakuji Mountain -|url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/020.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}}</ref><ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a">{{Cite web|url=https://mydaytrip.com/discover/location/hayatani-shrine|access-date=2023-12-02|website=mydaytrip.com|title=Discover Hayatani Shrine}}</ref> but due to the support of the [[Taira clan]] it was eclipsed.<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a" /> The two shrines used to simultaneously provide offerings in a single ceremony.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Havens|first1=Norman|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami|last2=Inoue|first2=Nobutaka|date=2006|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University|isbn=978-4-905853-08-4|language=en}}</ref> [[Itsukushima Shrine]] is the [[Ichinomiya]],<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''", p. 3] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref> and [[Take Shrine]] is the [[Sōja shrine]]. <ref name=":1">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=shFDAQAAIAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima|title=The Japan Daily Mail|date=1899|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VB9CAQAAMAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima|title=Japan Weekly Mail|date=1899|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Take Shrine Treasure House - Hiroshimatatemonogatari|url=https://www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp/soshiki_file/tatemonogatari/en/page062.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp}}</ref>
Hayatani Shrine is a sacred place in [[:en:Aki Province|Aki Province]] that has been worshipped for over 1,700 years. It was listed as a [[:en:Myojin Taisha|Myojin Taisha]] before the Engishiki was written, In 811 it and [[Itsukushima]] were both given the Rank by the Emperor.<ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-03-10|title=Myōjin {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|access-date=2023-12-02|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230310155140/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|archive-date=2023-03-10}}</ref> People from all over Japan visit Hayatani Shrine to pray for [[:en:Road traffic safety|traffic safety]], especially when buying a new car.<ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a" /> == Shikinaisha (式内社) == {| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff; font-size:85%" ! style="background:#a0f0a0" colspan=4 | {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} ! All three of these shrines are Myojin Taisha.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web|url=https://aki-fuchu.jp/english/introduction_e_right.htm|access-date=2023-12-02|website=aki-fuchu.jp}}</ref><ref name=":4" /> These are called the "Three Great Shrines of [[:en:Aki Province|Aki Province]]" and include [[Hayatani Shrine]], [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Take Shrine]].<ref name=":4">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LPq-lhGqzgEC&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=Hiroshima|date=1986|publisher=Hiroshima City|language=en}}</ref>

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11451199]]
[[:ja:安芸国の式内社一覧]]
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 22:02, 12 July 2025 (UTC)}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11451199]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Redirect|Three Great Shrines of Aki Province}} This is a '''List of Shikinaisha in Aki Province'''. Or the list of shrines in [[:en:Aki Province|Aki Province]] which is present in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}. On this page we will also cover the {{ill|Shikigeisha|ja|式外社|1=Shikigeisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikigeisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikigeisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of [[:en:Aki Province|Aki Province]] which are shrines that are known to have existed at that time due to other historical records, such as the [[Rikkokushi]], but for some reason were excluded from the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}. We will cover them in a separate list to avoid confusion.
This article is a list of shrines in Aki Province that are recorded in Volume 9 and Volume 10, ''{{ill|Engishiki|ja|Engishiki|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ({{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}). It comprises three shrines all highly Ranked as Myojin Taisha.
There are three such listed shrines. All three of these shrines are Myojin Taisha.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web|url=https://aki-fuchu.jp/english/introduction_e_right.htm|access-date=2023-12-02|website=aki-fuchu.jp}}</ref><ref name=":4" /> These are called the "Three Great Shrines of [[:en:Aki Province|Aki Province]]" and include [[Hayatani Shrine]], [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Take Shrine]].<ref name=":4">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LPq-lhGqzgEC&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=Hiroshima|date=1986|publisher=Hiroshima City|language=en}}</ref>
Take Shrine used to have a higher status than [[Itsukushima Shrine]],<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a">{{Cite web|title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Gokurakuji Mountain -|url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/020.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}}</ref><ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a">{{Cite web|url=https://mydaytrip.com/discover/location/hayatani-shrine|access-date=2023-12-02|website=mydaytrip.com|title=Discover Hayatani Shrine}}</ref> but due to the support of the [[Taira clan]] it was eclipsed.<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a" /> The two shrines used to simultaneously provide offerings in a single ceremony.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Havens|first1=Norman|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami|last2=Inoue|first2=Nobutaka|date=2006|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University|isbn=978-4-905853-08-4|language=en}}</ref> [[Itsukushima Shrine]] is the [[Ichinomiya]],<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''", p. 3] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref> and [[Take Shrine]] is the [[Sōja shrine]]. <ref name=":1">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=shFDAQAAIAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima|title=The Japan Daily Mail|date=1899|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VB9CAQAAMAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima|title=Japan Weekly Mail|date=1899|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Take Shrine Treasure House - Hiroshimatatemonogatari|url=https://www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp/soshiki_file/tatemonogatari/en/page062.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp}}</ref>
Hayatani Shrine is a sacred place in [[:en:Aki Province|Aki Province]] that has been worshipped for over 1,700 years. It was listed as a [[:en:Myojin Taisha|Myojin Taisha]] before the Engishiki was written, In 811 it and [[Itsukushima]] were both given the Rank by the Emperor.<ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-03-10|title=Myōjin {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|access-date=2023-12-02|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230310155140/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|archive-date=2023-03-10}}</ref> People from all over Japan visit Hayatani Shrine to pray for [[:en:Road traffic safety|traffic safety]], especially when buying a new car.<ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a" /> == Shikinaisha (式内社) == {| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff; font-size:85%" ! Hayatani Shrine is a sacred place in [[:en:Aki Province|Aki Province]] that has been worshipped for over 1,700 years. It was listed as a [[:en:Myojin Taisha|Myojin Taisha]] before the Engishiki was written, In 811 it and [[Itsukushima]] were both given the Rank by the Emperor.<ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-03-10|title=Myōjin {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|access-date=2023-12-02|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230310155140/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|archive-date=2023-03-10}}</ref> People from all over Japan visit Hayatani Shrine to pray for [[:en:Road traffic safety|traffic safety]], especially when buying a new car.<ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a" /> == Shikinaisha (式内社) == {| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff; font-size:85%" ! {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11451199]]
[[:ja:安芸国の式内社一覧]]
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 22:02, 12 July 2025 (UTC)}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11451199]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Redirect|Three Great Shrines of Aki Province}} This article is a list of shrines in [[Aki Province]] that are recorded in Volume 9 and Volume 10, ''{{ill|Engishiki|ja|Engishiki|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ({{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}). It comprises three shrines all highly Ranked as Myojin Taisha.
There are three such listed shrines. All three of these shrines are Myojin Taisha.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web|url=https://aki-fuchu.jp/english/introduction_e_right.htm|access-date=2023-12-02|website=aki-fuchu.jp}}</ref><ref name=":4" /> These are called the "Three Great Shrines of [[:en:Aki Province|Aki Province]]" and include [[Hayatani Shrine]], [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Take Shrine]].<ref name=":4">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LPq-lhGqzgEC&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=Hiroshima|date=1986|publisher=Hiroshima City|language=en}}</ref>
Take Shrine used to have a higher status than [[Itsukushima Shrine]],<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a">{{Cite web|title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Gokurakuji Mountain -|url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/020.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}}</ref><ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a">{{Cite web|url=https://mydaytrip.com/discover/location/hayatani-shrine|access-date=2023-12-02|website=mydaytrip.com|title=Discover Hayatani Shrine}}</ref> but due to the support of the [[Taira clan]] it was eclipsed.<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a" /> The two shrines used to simultaneously provide offerings in a single ceremony.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Havens|first1=Norman|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami|last2=Inoue|first2=Nobutaka|date=2006|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University|isbn=978-4-905853-08-4|language=en}}</ref> [[Itsukushima Shrine]] is the [[Ichinomiya]],<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''", p. 3] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref> and [[Take Shrine]] is the [[Sōja shrine]]. <ref name=":1">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=shFDAQAAIAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima|title=The Japan Daily Mail|date=1899|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VB9CAQAAMAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima|title=Japan Weekly Mail|date=1899|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Take Shrine Treasure House - Hiroshimatatemonogatari|url=https://www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp/soshiki_file/tatemonogatari/en/page062.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp}}</ref>

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11451199]]
[[:ja:安芸国の式内社一覧]]
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 22:02, 12 July 2025 (UTC)}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11451199]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Redirect|Three Great Shrines of Aki Province}} This article is a list of shrines in [[Aki Province]] that are recorded in Volume 9 and Volume 10, ''{{ill|Engishiki|ja|Engishiki|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ({{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}).
There are three shrines in this list. All three of these shrines are Myojin Taisha.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web|url=https://aki-fuchu.jp/english/introduction_e_right.htm|access-date=2023-12-02|website=aki-fuchu.jp}}</ref><ref name=":4" /> These are called the "Three Great Shrines of [[Aki Province]]" and include [[Hayatani Shrine]], [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Take Shrine]].<ref name=":4">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LPq-lhGqzgEC&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=Hiroshima|date=1986|publisher=Hiroshima City|language=en}}</ref>
Take Shrine used to have a higher status than [[Itsukushima Shrine]],<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a">{{Cite web|title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Gokurakuji Mountain -|url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/020.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}}</ref><ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a">{{Cite web|url=https://mydaytrip.com/discover/location/hayatani-shrine|access-date=2023-12-02|website=mydaytrip.com|title=Discover Hayatani Shrine}}</ref> but due to the support of the [[Taira clan]] it was eclipsed.<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a" /> The two shrines used to simultaneously provide offerings in a single ceremony.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Havens|first1=Norman|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami|last2=Inoue|first2=Nobutaka|date=2006|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University|isbn=978-4-905853-08-4|language=en}}</ref> [[Itsukushima Shrine]] is the [[Ichinomiya]],<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''", p. 3] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref> and [[Take Shrine]] is the [[Sōja shrine]]. <ref name=":1">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=shFDAQAAIAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima|title=The Japan Daily Mail|date=1899|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VB9CAQAAMAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima|title=Japan Weekly Mail|date=1899|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Take Shrine Treasure House - Hiroshimatatemonogatari|url=https://www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp/soshiki_file/tatemonogatari/en/page062.html|access-date=2023-12-02|website=www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp}}</ref>
Hayatani Shrine is a sacred place in [[:en:Aki Province|Aki Province]] that has been worshipped for over 1,700 years. It was listed as a [[:en:Myojin Taisha|Myojin Taisha]] before the Engishiki was written, In 811 it and [[Itsukushima]] were both given the Rank by the Emperor.<ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-03-10|title=Myōjin {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|access-date=2023-12-02|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230310155140/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9984|archive-date=2023-03-10}}</ref> People from all over Japan visit Hayatani Shrine to pray for [[:en:Road traffic safety|traffic safety]], especially when buying a new car.<ref name="mydaytripcom-2023a" /> == Shikinaisha (式内社) == {| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff; font-size:85%" ! style="background:#a0f0a0" colspan=4 | {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} ! style="background:#a0f0a0" colspan=3 | Designated Shrines ! style="background:#a0f0a0" rowspan=2 | Compilation |- style="white-space:nowrap;" ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.6em; padding-left:2.6em;" | Shrine Name ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.1em; padding-left:2.1em;" | Reading ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.1em; padding-left:2.1em;" | Rank ! style="background:#a0f0a0;" | Note ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.6em; padding-left:2.6em;" | Shrine Name ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.1em; padding-left:2.1em;" | Location ! style="background:#a0f0a0; padding-right:2.3em; padding-left:2.3em;" | Remarks |- |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''[[:en:Saeki_District,_Hiroshima|Saeki]]''' 2 seats |- |'''速谷神社'''||hayatanino||Myojin Taisha<ref name=":5">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QmdRAAAAYAAJ&q=Hayatani+Shrine|title=Engi-shiki; Procedures of the Engi Era: Books VI-X|date=1972|publisher=Sophia University|language=en}}</ref>||Tsukinami-/Niiname-sai||[[Hayatani Shrine]]||[[:en:Hatsukaichi|Hatsukaichi]], a suburb of [[:en:Hiroshima|Hiroshima]]<ref>{{Cite web|date=2019-03-03|title=Hayatani Jinja|url=http://yuugenguide.squarespace.com/shrines/2018/12/20/hayatani-jinja|access-date=2023-12-02|website=Yuugen 幽玄|language=en-US}}</ref>||[[:en:Ninomiya|Ninomiya]]<ref name="wwwhiroshimabunkajp-2023a" />||[https://jmapps.ne.jp/kokugakuin/det.html?data_id=183120 archive] |- |'''伊都伎島神社'''||itsukishimano||Myojin Taisha||||[[Itsukushima Shrine]]||[[:en:Hatsukaichi|Hatsukaichi]], a suburb of [[:en:Hiroshima|Hiroshima]]<ref name="cali-2013a">{{Cite book|jstor=j.ctt6wqfhm|title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion|last1=Cali|first1=Joseph|last2=Dougill|first2=John|last3=Ciotti|first3=Geoff|date=2013|publisher=University of Hawai'i Press|isbn=9780824837136}}</ref>||[[Ichinomiya]]||[https://jmapps.ne.jp/kokugakuin/det.html?data_id=183121 archive] |- |colspan=8 style="background-color:#ccffcc;"|'''[[Aki District, Hiroshima|Aki]]''' 1 seat |- |'''多家神社'''||ohoiheno||Myojin Taisha<ref name=":3" />|| ||[[Take Shrine]]||[[:en:Fuchū, Hiroshima (town)|Fuchu]], [[Aki District, Hiroshima]]||[[Sōja shrine]]<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":0" />||[https://jmapps.ne.jp/kokugakuin/det.html?data_id=183122 archive] |}
